# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> **Explain the concept of "Plant Location" and list any FOUR primary factors that influence the plant location decision.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Plant Location is the strategic decision of selecting the geographic region where an industrial facility will be established. It is a long-term, capital-intensive, and largely irreversible decision.

**Four Primary Factors (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Proximity to Markets** — Reduces outbound transportation cost and improves customer service.
2. **Availability of Raw Materials** — Reduces inbound logistics cost; especially critical for weight-losing industries (e.g., sugar, cement).
3. **Transportation Infrastructure** — Road, rail, port, and airport connectivity for both inputs and outputs.
4. **Government Policies** — Tax holidays, subsidies, SEZ benefits, and ease of doing business.

*(Alternative valid factors: Labour availability, Power & Water supply, Climatic conditions.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **Distinguish between Plant Location and Site Selection with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Plant Location | Site Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Macro-level (region/city) | Micro-level (specific plot) |
| **Decision Maker** | Top Management / Board | Project Engineer + Site Team |
| **Example** | Choosing between Kerala vs Karnataka for a new IT park | Choosing the exact 50-acre plot within Bengaluru for the IT park |
| **Focus** | Markets, labour, policy, infrastructure | Soil, drainage, topography, local zoning |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** Plant location is the "**where in the country**" decision; site selection is the "**where on the map**" decision. The former determines cost structure; the latter determines constructability and safety.

---

## 📝 Part B — Full ESE Question (14 Marks, Module 1 Internal Choice)

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 1]**

> **(a) Explain the Factor Rating Method for plant location. Mention its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Factor Rating Method (also called Weighted Point Method) is a quantitative technique that evaluates multiple candidate locations by assigning weights to key locational factors and rating each location on a common scale.

**Steps (3 marks):**
1. Identify the key factors influencing location (e.g., market, raw material, labour, power, transport, climate).
2. Assign a relative weight $w_i$ to each factor such that $\sum w_i = 1$ (or 100%).
3. Rate each location $j$ on each factor $i$ on a common scale (e.g., 1–10).
4. Compute weighted score: $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$.
5. Select the location with the **maximum weighted score**.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Incorporates both quantitative and qualitative factors.
- Simple, transparent, and easy to communicate to management.
- Allows sensitivity analysis by varying weights.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Subjectivity in weight assignment.
- Choice of rating scale (1–5 vs 1–10) affects the result.
- Cannot capture inter-factor dependencies.

---

> **(b) A company is evaluating three locations A, B, and C for setting up a manufacturing plant. The relevant factors and their weights along with ratings (on a 1–10 scale) are given below. Determine the best location. (7 marks)**

| Factor | Weight | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to Market | 0.35 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Raw Material | 0.25 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Labour | 0.20 | 8 | 8 | 6 |
| Power & Utilities | 0.10 | 7 | 6 | 9 |
| Transport | 0.10 | 8 | 7 | 8 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify weights sum (0.5 mark):**
$$0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute $S_A$ (2 marks):**
$$S_A = (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8)$$
$$S_A = 3.15 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 0.70 + 0.80 = 7.75$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_B$ (2 marks):**
$$S_B = (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 7)$$
$$S_B = 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 0.60 + 0.70 = 7.60$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S_C$ (1.5 marks):**
$$S_C = (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8)$$
$$S_C = 2.80 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 7.45$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark):**
$$\max(7.75,\; 7.60,\; 7.45) = 7.75 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Location A is best}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Weights not summing to 1.00** — A common error. Always verify $\sum w_i = 1$ before computing scores (deduct 0.5 mark if skipped).
> 2. **Wrong rounding or transcription error** — Write the final weighted score to **2 decimal places**.
> 3. **No justification for tie-breaking** — If two scores tie, explain the tie-breaker logic.
> 4. **Forgetting units or dimensionless nature of the score** — Mention that the score is **dimensionless**.

---

### ✦ Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 1]**

> **(a) Explain the Break-Even Analysis method for plant location. Derive the formula for crossover volume. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (1 mark):** Break-Even Analysis (also called Cost-Volume Analysis) compares two or more candidate locations by equating their total cost equations to find the production volume at which they are equally costly.

**Total Cost Equation (2 marks):**

$$TC = FC + v \cdot Q$$

Where $FC$ = Annual Fixed Cost, $v$ = Variable Cost per unit, $Q$ = Annual Production Volume.

**Derivation of Crossover Volume (3 marks):**

Equating $TC_A = TC_B$:

$$FC_A + v_A \cdot Q = FC_B + v_B \cdot Q$$

Rearranging:
$$FC_A - FC_B = v_B \cdot Q - v_A \cdot Q$$
$$FC_A - FC_B = Q(v_B - v_A)$$
$$\boxed{Q^* = \frac{FC_A - FC_B}{v_B - v_A}}$$

**Decision Rule (1 mark):** If $Q > Q^*$, choose location with lower $v$; if $Q < Q^*$, choose location with lower $FC$.

---

> **(b) A firm is choosing between Location P and Location Q. Fixed and variable costs are given. Find the crossover volume and state which location is preferred for an annual demand of 60,000 units. (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Location P | Location Q |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed Cost (₹/year) | 40,00,000 | 25,00,000 |
| Variable Cost (₹/unit) | 180 | 220 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write Total Cost Equations (1 mark):**
$$TC_P = 40{,}00{,}000 + 180Q$$
$$TC_Q = 25{,}00{,}000 + 220Q$$

**Step 2 — Equate and Solve for $Q^*$ (3 marks):**
$$40{,}00{,}000 + 180Q = 25{,}00{,}000 + 220Q$$
$$40{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = 220Q - 180Q$$
$$15{,}00{,}000 = 40Q$$
$$\boxed{Q^* = 37{,}500 \text{ units/year}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $Q = 60{,}000$ (2 marks):**
$$TC_P = 40{,}00{,}000 + 180(60{,}000) = 40{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}48{,}00{,}000$$
$$TC_Q = 25{,}00{,}000 + 220(60{,}000) = 25{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}32{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}57{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Decision (1 mark):**
$$TC_P = 1.48\text{ Cr} < TC_Q = 1.57\text{ Cr} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Choose Location P}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compare total costs** at the actual demand — students often stop at $Q^*$ without verifying which side of crossover the actual volume lies on (deduct 1 mark).
> 2. **Sign error** when subtracting FCs and VCs — use brackets carefully.
> 3. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly** — write the comparison explicitly with proper ₹ symbols.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**
>
> - **Plant Location** = Macro / regional decision; **Site Selection** = Micro / plot-level decision.
> - Plant location is a **strategic, irreversible, capital-intensive** decision.
> - **Factor Rating Method** uses weights + ratings → **weighted score** = $\sum w_i r_{ij}$.
> - Always verify $\sum w_i = 1.00$ (or 100%) before computing.
> - **Break-Even Analysis** uses $TC = FC + vQ$; crossover volume $Q^* = \frac{\Delta FC}{\Delta v}$.
> - Decision Rule: $Q > Q^*$ → choose **low VC** location; $Q < Q^*$ → choose **low FC** location.
> - **Centre of Gravity** gives (x, y) coordinates for single-facility location: $C_x = \frac{\sum d_{ix} Q_i}{\sum Q_i}$.
> - **Transportation Model** is an LP problem minimizing $\sum \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$.
> - **Orientation Types** (very high-yield):
>   - **Raw Material Oriented** → Cement, Sugar (weight-losing in process)
>   - **Market Oriented** → Bottling, Bakery (perishable / costly-to-ship)
>   - **Power Oriented** → Aluminium smelting
>   - **Labour Oriented** → Software parks, textiles
>   - **Transport Oriented** → Petroleum refineries, shipyards
> - **Subsoil water table, soil bearing capacity, drainage** are top site-selection factors.
> - For KTU ESE, always show **formula + substitution + result** for full marks.
> - In Factor Rating, if two locations tie, mention a **tie-breaker** (sensitivity analysis, qualitative factors).
> - **Government policy** & **environmental clearance** are increasingly critical in 2024 Scheme answers.
> - Remember: **Plant Location decisions cannot be undone cheaply — they shape 30+ years of operations.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Data for Construction Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT631 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Data for Construction Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preliminary Data for Construction Projects** refers to the systematic collection, analysis, and documentation of all essential information required *before* the actual execution of a construction project begins. This dataset forms the foundational intelligence layer upon which feasibility decisions, design choices, cost estimates, schedules, and statutory approvals are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Preliminary data encompasses the cluster of investigations, surveys, and studies — including site conditions, market dynamics, regulatory environment, and resource availability — that collectively validate the technical, financial, and legal viability of a proposed construction project.*

The four pillars of preliminary data classification in the KTU 2024 framework are:

| Pillar | Description | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical Data** | Site topography, soil profile, hydrology, climate | Site Investigation Report |
| **Economic Data** | Market demand, financing options, cost indices | Feasibility Report |
| **Legal/Statutory Data** | Zoning laws, building codes, environmental clearances | Compliance Checklist |
| **Managerial Data** | Stakeholder mapping, resource pool, contractor availability | Project Charter Draft |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient before prescribing medicine. The doctor does not just guess — they collect **preliminary data**: blood pressure, temperature, medical history, allergies, lifestyle habits. Only after this **data triage** is complete can an accurate diagnosis (feasibility) and treatment plan (project execution) be formulated.

In the same way, a construction project without proper preliminary data is like a surgeon operating blindfolded. A building constructed on unknown soil might crack; a high-rise planned without wind data might sway dangerously; a commercial complex built in a non-viable market will fail commercially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The House-Buying Analogy:**
> Before buying a house, a smart buyer checks: location, soil test report, legal clearances, nearby amenities, resale value, and loan eligibility. **All of this is "preliminary data"** — the same discipline, scaled up, applies to mega construction projects.

### 1.3 Critical Terminology (KTU Standardized)

- **Site Reconnaissance:** A preliminary visual walkthrough of the proposed site.
- **Feasibility Study:** A comprehensive evaluation to determine project viability.
- **Topographical Survey:** Mapping of surface features, elevations, and contours.
- **Geotechnical Investigation:** Subsurface soil and rock characterization.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Study of ecological and societal consequences.
- **Building Bye-Laws:** Local municipal regulations governing construction.
- **DPR (Detailed Project Report):** The consolidated document of all preliminary data findings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Preliminary Data

The data collection process is structured in a **funnel hierarchy**, starting broad and narrowing to specifics.

**Level 1 — Macro-Level Data (Regional/National Context)**
- Climatic conditions of the region
- Regional economic indicators (GDP, inflation, demand-supply)
- National building code (NBC 2016 in India) compliance
- Availability of national-level infrastructure (roads, ports, power)

**Level 2 — Meso-Level Data (Locality/City Context)**
- Municipal zoning maps and land-use plans
- Local bye-laws and FAR (Floor Area Ratio) norms
- Local contractor and labor availability
- Proximity to suppliers and markets

**Level 3 — Micro-Level Data (Project Site Context)**
- Exact site topography and boundary demarcation
- Soil bearing capacity and water table depth
- Seismic zone classification
- Existing utilities (water, sewage, electricity, gas)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for Data Acquisition

The KTU 2024 curriculum prescribes the following logical flow:

- **Step 1 — Project Conception:** Identification of need and conceptual brief.
- **Step 2 — Preliminary Survey:** Desktop research and rough cost estimate (±25% accuracy).
- **Step 3 — Detailed Site Investigation:** Field surveys, soil testing, hydrological studies.
- **Step 4 — Feasibility Analysis:** Technical + Economic + Legal + Schedule (T.E.L.S.) evaluation.
- **Step 5 — Detailed Project Report (DPR) Compilation:** Final document preparation.
- **Step 6 — Statutory Approvals:** Municipal, environmental, and fire clearances.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always remember the acronym **T.E.L.S.** for Feasibility Analysis. KTU examiners frequently test this in 2-mark questions.

### 2.3 Why Each Data Type Matters

| Data Type | Engineering Justification | Consequence of Ignoring |
|---|---|---|
| **Soil Test Data** | Determines foundation type, depth, and bearing capacity | Structural failure, differential settlement |
| **Topographical Data** | Influences cut-fill balance, drainage design | Flooding, earthwork cost overruns |
| **Hydrological Data** | Sizes stormwater drains and flood protection | Site inundation, foundation damage |
| **Meteorological Data** | Influences building orientation, HVAC sizing, wind loads | Energy inefficiency, structural instability |
| **Seismic Data** | Defines earthquake-resistant design parameters | Collapse during earthquakes |
| **Market Data** | Validates commercial viability of the project | Project becomes a financial liability |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While preliminary data is largely qualitative, several **quantitative metrics** are essential:

| Metric | Formula / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Floor Area Ratio (FAR)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Total Plot Area}}$ | Statutory compliance |
| **Ground Coverage Ratio** | $\text{GCR} = \dfrac{\text{Ground Floor Covered Area}}{\text{Total Plot Area}}$ | Open space regulation |
| **Building Height Index** | $\text{BHI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area} \times \text{Number of Floors}}$ | Density check |
| **Soil Bearing Capacity (SBC)** | $\text{SBC} = \dfrac{\text{Load at Failure}}{\text{Area of Footing}}$ ($\text{kN/m}^2$) | Foundation design |
| **Cut-Fill Volume Balance** | $V_{\text{cut}} \approx V_{\text{fill}} \pm e$ where $e$ = earthwork shrinkage factor | Earthwork economy |
| **Site Triangulation Distance** | $D = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ ($\text{m}$) | Topographical mapping |
| **Construction Cost Index (CCI)** | $\text{CCI} = \dfrac{\text{Current Period Cost}}{\text{Base Period Cost}} \times 100$ | Inflation adjustment |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$ ($\%$) | Economic feasibility |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Long-term viability |
| **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{PV of Benefits}}{\sum \text{PV of Costs}}$ | Public project screening |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Always express NPV and BCR in present-value terms using a discount rate $r$ (typically **8–12%** in Indian public infrastructure projects).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The preliminary data framework is the **backbone of every civil engineering discipline**:

- In **Structural Engineering:** Soil data dictates foundation design; seismic data dictates lateral load design.
- In **Transportation Engineering:** Traffic data and topographical data govern highway alignment and gradient design.
- In **Environmental Engineering:** Hydrological data governs drainage and water treatment plant sizing.
- In **Construction Management:** Market data governs tender pricing, contractor selection, and project scheduling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Cut-Fill Volume Calculation

In preliminary earthwork estimation, the cut-fill volume between two successive contour lines is derived using the **Trapezoidal (Average End Area) Method**.

**Given:**
- Cross-sectional area at Section 1: $A_1$
- Cross-sectional area at Section 2: $A_2$
- Horizontal distance between sections: $L$

**Derivation Steps:**

The volume of earthwork between two cross-sections approximates a prismatoid. The average of the two end-areas, multiplied by the distance between them, gives the volume.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{segment}} &= \left(\dfrac{A_1 + A_2}{2}\right) \times L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total cut-fill volume** across the entire site is the sum of all such segments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{A_{i} + A_{i+1}}{2}\right) \times L_{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
- $A_1$ and $A_2$ are computed from cross-section surveys at two consecutive stations.
- The arithmetic mean $\frac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2}$ approximates the average cross-section.
- Multiplying by $L$ converts the area into a volume.
- Summing across $n$ stations gives the total cut or fill volume.

> [!NOTE]
> The shrinkage factor $s$ (typically **0.85 to 0.95** for soils) must be applied: $V_{\text{compacted}} = s \times V_{\text{loose}}$.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Net Present Value (NPV) for Project Feasibility

**Given:**
- Initial investment at $t = 0$: $C_0$ (negative cash flow)
- Annual cash inflows: $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$
- Project life: $n$ years
- Discount rate: $r$

**Derivation Steps:**

The NPV is the sum of all discounted cash flows over the project lifetime.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= C_0 + \dfrac{C_1}{(1+r)^1} + \dfrac{C_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{C_n}{(1+r)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

In compact sigma notation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Rule:**
- $\text{NPV} \geq 0$: Project is **economically viable** ✓
- $\text{NPV} < 0$: Project is **not viable** ✗

**Worked Example (KTU Standard):**

A construction project requires an initial investment of **₹50,00,000** and generates annual returns of **₹12,00,000** for **6 years**. Assume discount rate $r = 10\%$.

**Step 1:** Write down all cash flows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= -50{,}00{,}000 \\
C_1 = C_2 = \cdots = C_6 &= 12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the NPV formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -50{,}00{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{6} \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the discount factors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^1 &= 1.1000 \\
(1.10)^2 &= 1.2100 \\
(1.10)^3 &= 1.3310 \\
(1.10)^4 &= 1.4641 \\
(1.10)^5 &= 1.6105 \\
(1.10)^6 &= 1.7716
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the present value of each year's inflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}_1 &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.1000} = 10{,}90{,}909.09 \\
\text{PV}_2 &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.2100} = 9{,}91{,}735.54 \\
\text{PV}_3 &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.3310} = 9{,}01{,}577.76 \\
\text{PV}_4 &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} = 8{,}19{,}616.14 \\
\text{PV}_5 &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.6105} = 7{,}45{,}105.59 \\
\text{PV}_6 &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.7716} = 6{,}77{,}368.72
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum all present values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \text{PV} &= 10{,}90{,}909.09 + 9{,}91{,}735.54 + 9{,}01{,}577.76 + 8{,}19{,}616.14 + 7{,}45{,}105.59 + 6{,}77{,}368.72 \\
&= 52{,}26{,}312.84
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute NPV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -50{,}00{,}000 + 52{,}26{,}312.84 = +2{,}26{,}312.84
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since NPV > 0, the project is **economically feasible** at a 10% discount rate.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Soil Bearing Capacity Approximation (Terzaghi's Principle)

For preliminary foundation design, Terzaghi's ultimate bearing capacity formula for a strip footing is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{ult}} &= c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $c$ = cohesion of soil ($\text{kN/m}^2$)
- $q$ = overburden pressure ($= \gamma \cdot D_f$) ($\text{kN/m}^2$)
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil ($\text{kN/m}^3$)
- $B$ = width of footing ($\text{m}$)
- $D_f$ = depth of foundation ($\text{m}$)
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (dimensionless, function of $\phi$)

**Allowable Bearing Capacity (SBC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{allow}} &= \dfrac{q_{\text{ult}}}{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $F$ = Factor of Safety (typically **2.5 to 3.0**).

**Worked Example (KTU Standard):**

Given a strip footing with:
- $c = 15$ $\text{kN/m}^2$
- $\phi = 30°$ (so $N_c = 30.14$, $N_q = 18.40$, $N_\gamma = 22.40$)
- $\gamma = 18$ $\text{kN/m}^3$
- $D_f = 1.5$ $\text{m}$
- $B = 2.0$ $\text{m}$
- $F = 3$

**Step 1:** Compute overburden pressure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \gamma \cdot D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term of $q_{\text{ult}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1} &= c \cdot N_c = 15 \times 30.14 = 452.10 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\text{Term 2} &= q \cdot N_q = 27 \times 18.40 = 496.80 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\text{Term 3} &= 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma = 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.0 \times 22.40 = 403.20 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum to get $q_{\text{ult}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{ult}} &= 452.10 + 496.80 + 403.20 = 1352.10 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute allowable bearing capacity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{allow}} &= \dfrac{1352.10}{3} = 450.70 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This preliminary SBC value directly informs the **size of the foundation** in the DPR.

### 3.4 KTU 3-Mark & 14-Mark Analytical Decision Matrix

| Parameter | Preliminary Data Required | Engineering Application | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Site Topography** | Contour map, slope analysis | Cut-fill balance, drainage | High (14-mark Qs) |
| **Soil Profile** | Borehole data, SPT 'N' values | Foundation type, depth | High (14-mark Qs) |
| **Water Table** | Seasonal variation data | Dewatering, basement design | Medium (3-mark) |
| **Seismic Zone** | IS 1893 classification | Lateral load design | High (14-mark Qs) |
| **Market Demand** | Demographic + commercial data | Project type selection | Medium (14-mark Qs) |
| **Statutory** | Local bye-laws, FAR/FSI | Design compliance | High (3-mark & 14-mark) |
| **Climate** | Temperature, rainfall, wind | Material selection, HVAC | Medium (3-mark) |
| **Financial** | Cost indices, ROI, NPV | Project sanctioning | High (14-mark Qs) |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. List any six types of preliminary data required before the start of a construction project.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Topographical data** — Contour maps, elevations, and slope details of the site.
2. **Geotechnical data** — Soil type, bearing capacity, and water table depth.
3. **Hydrological data** — Rainfall intensity, flood levels, and drainage patterns.
4. **Meteorological data** — Temperature, humidity, wind speed, and direction.
5. **Seismic data** — Classification of the site into seismic zones (as per IS 1893).
6. **Market and economic data** — Demand analysis, cost indices, and financing options.
7. **Statutory data** — Local building bye-laws, FAR norms, and environmental clearances.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2. What is a Feasibility Study? Explain the T.E.L.S. framework briefly.**

**Model Answer (Definition 1.5 marks + TELS 1.5 marks):**
A **Feasibility Study** is a comprehensive evaluation of a proposed construction project to determine whether it is technically, economically, legally, and schedule-wise viable before significant capital is committed.
- **T — Technical Feasibility:** Whether the project can be engineered with available technology and site conditions.
- **E — Economic Feasibility:** Whether the project is financially justifiable using NPV, ROI, BCR.
- **L — Legal Feasibility:** Whether the project complies with zoning, environmental, and statutory norms.
- **S — Schedule Feasibility:** Whether the project can be completed within the required timeframe.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Q3 (a). Explain in detail the various types of preliminary data required for a construction project, with emphasis on site investigation data. (7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b). A construction project requires an initial investment of ₹80 lakhs and is expected to generate annual cash inflows of ₹18 lakhs for 8 years. The discount rate is 12%. Calculate the NPV and comment on the project's feasibility. (7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Q3 (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Preliminary data forms the **intelligence backbone** of any construction project. Without accurate preliminary data, projects suffer from cost overruns, delays, and even structural failures.

**Body — Detailed Classification (5 marks):**

**(i) Topographical Data (1 mark):**
- Includes contour mapping, slope analysis, and existing feature identification.
- **Tools used:** Total station, GPS, drone-based LiDAR surveys.
- **Output:** Digital Elevation Model (DEM) used for cut-fill estimation and drainage design.

**(ii) Geotechnical Data (1 mark):**
- Includes soil profile, Standard Penetration Test (SPT) 'N' values, and Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC).
- **Tools used:** Borehole drilling, plate load test, SPT, CPT.
- **Output:** Foundation type and depth recommendation.

**(iii) Hydrological Data (1 mark):**
- Includes rainfall data, flood levels, groundwater table depth, and runoff coefficients.
- **Tools used:** Rain gauges, piezometers, hydrological modeling software (e.g., HEC-HMS).
- **Output:** Stormwater drainage design and flood protection measures.

**(iv) Meteorological Data (0.5 mark):**
- Includes temperature, humidity, wind speed/direction, and solar radiation.
- **Output:** HVAC sizing, building orientation, material durability decisions.

**(v) Seismic and Environmental Data (0.5 mark):**
- Seismic zone classification (IS 1893), Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) clearance.
- **Output:** Seismic design coefficients, environmental compliance.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A systematic site investigation combining all the above data types ensures that the Detailed Project Report (DPR) is **technically sound, economically viable, and legally compliant**, thereby minimizing risks during execution.

---

### Model Answer for Q3 (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Initial investment $C_0 = -80{,}00{,}000$ (₹80 lakhs)
- Annual cash inflow $C_t = 18{,}00{,}000$ for $t = 1$ to $8$
- Discount rate $r = 12\% = 0.12$
- Project life $n = 8$ years

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the NPV formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the discount factors $(1.12)^t$ for $t = 1$ to $8$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.4049 \\
(1.12)^4 &= 1.5735 \\
(1.12)^5 &= 1.7623 \\
(1.12)^6 &= 1.9738 \\
(1.12)^7 &= 2.2107 \\
(1.12)^8 &= 2.4760
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute present value of each year's inflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}_1 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.1200} = 16{,}07{,}142.86 \\
\text{PV}_2 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.2544} = 14{,}34{,}949.87 \\
\text{PV}_3 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.4049} = 12{,}81{,}205.24 \\
\text{PV}_4 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.5735} = 11{,}43{,}962.36 \\
\text{PV}_5 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.7623} = 10{,}21{,}394.07 \\
\text{PV}_6 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.9738} = 9{,}12{,}038.45 \\
\text{PV}_7 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{2.2107} = 8{,}14{,}319.15 \\
\text{PV}_8 &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{2.4760} = 7{,}27{,}070.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Sum all present values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \text{PV} &= 89{,}41{,}082.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute NPV and conclude.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -80{,}00{,}000 + 89{,}41{,}082.67 = +9{,}41{,}082.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\text{NPV} = +₹9{,}41{,}082.67 > 0$, the project is **economically feasible** and should be sanctioned.

---

> **OR (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> **Q4 (a). Discuss the role of a Detailed Project Report (DPR) in a construction project. What are the key components of a DPR? (7 marks)**
>
> **Q4 (b). Explain the importance of soil investigation in construction projects. List the various field tests conducted for soil exploration. (7 marks)**

### Model Answer Outline for Q4 (a) — 7 Marks

**Role of DPR (3 marks):**
- Acts as the **single source of truth** for all stakeholders.
- Serves as the **bankable document** for securing loans and approvals.
- Forms the **baseline** for project monitoring, scope control, and change management.

**Key Components (4 marks):**
1. Executive Summary
2. Project Background and Justification
3. Site Analysis (topography, soil, hydrology)
4. Technical Design Basis
5. Cost Estimates (capital + operational)
6. Financial Analysis (NPV, IRR, BCR, payback period)
7. Implementation Schedule
8. Statutory and Environmental Compliance
9. Risk Analysis and Mitigation
10. Appendices (drawings, data sheets, survey reports)

### Model Answer Outline for Q4 (b) — 7 Marks

**Importance of Soil Investigation (3 marks):**
- Determines the **type and depth of foundation**.
- Ensures **structural safety** and prevents differential settlement.
- Helps estimate **earthwork quantities** and dewatering requirements.
- Mitigates **geotechnical risks** such as liquefaction, expansive soils, and slope failure.

**Field Tests (4 marks):**
- **Test Pits** — shallow visual examination.
- **Borehole Drilling** — obtains soil samples at depth.
- **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** — measures soil density and consistency via 'N' value.
- **Cone Penetration Test (CPT)** — continuous profile of soil resistance.
- **Plate Load Test** — determines in-situ bearing capacity.
- **Vane Shear Test** — measures undrained shear strength of cohesive soils.
- **Pumping Test** — determines permeability and groundwater yield.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up SBC and Allowable Bearing Pressure** — Always divide ultimate capacity by Factor of Safety ($F \geq 3$).
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical NPV/scheduling problems — leads to **0 marks** despite correct logic.
> 3. **Confusing NPV sign convention** — Outflows are negative, inflows are positive.
> 4. **Skipping discount factor computation** — Examiners expect *all eight years* of factors shown explicitly.
> 5. **Not linking preliminary data to specific engineering decisions** — Always answer with the *application* in mind, not just the *definition*.
> 6. **Forgetting T.E.L.S. expansion** — A 2-mark question can become 0 if you write only "T, E, L, S" without expansion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Preliminary Data for Construction Projects**

- **Definition:** Preliminary data is the foundational dataset (technical, economic, legal, managerial) gathered before construction begins.
- **Primary Objective:** Validate project viability and minimize execution risks.
- **Key Categories:** Topographical, Geotechnical, Hydrological, Meteorological, Seismic, Market, Statutory, Financial.
- **Feasibility Framework:** **T.E.L.S.** — Technical, Economic, Legal, Schedule.
- **Critical Documents:** Site Investigation Report, Detailed Project Report (DPR), EIA Report, Building Bye-Laws Compliance.
- **FAR Formula:** $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Total Plot Area}}$.
- **SBC Formula:** $q_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{q_{\text{ult}}}{F}$, where $F = 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0$.
- **NPV Rule:** $\text{NPV} \geq 0$ → Project is **viable**; $\text{NPV} < 0$ → **Reject**.
- **Cut-Fill Volume:** $V = \left(\dfrac{A_1 + A_2}{2}\right) \times L$ (Trapezoidal Method).
- **Discount Rate Range:** **8–12%** for typical Indian construction projects.
- **Shrinkage Factor:** $0.85 \text{ to } 0.95$ for loose-to-compacted soil conversion.
- **Key Indian Standards:** IS 1893 (Seismic), IS 6403 (Bearing Capacity), NBC 2016 (National Building Code), IS 2720 (Soil Testing).
- **Seismic Zones in India:** II to V — Kerala falls mainly in **Zone III** (moderate risk).
- **Approval Authorities:** Local Municipality, State Environmental Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA), Fire & Rescue, Pollution Control Board.
- **Decision Gate:** A feasibility study is the **decision gate** that determines Go/No-Go for a project.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Engineering: Flow Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT631 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Engineering: Flow Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Process Engineering?

**Process Engineering** is the branch of engineering that designs, controls, and optimizes industrial chemical, physical, and biological processes. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Code: PEPOT631), it is treated as the *technical heart* of any capital project — translating laboratory chemistry into safe, profitable, full-scale plant operations.

The principal deliverable of process engineering is a set of **Flow Diagrams** — standardized graphical documents that communicate how raw materials are transformed into finished products through interconnected unit operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *Process Engineering* encompasses the development of process flow schemes, the preparation of material and energy balances, the selection and sizing of process equipment, and the generation of Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs) and Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs) that form the contractual and engineering basis for plant construction.

## 1.2 What are Flow Diagrams?

A **Flow Diagram** is a scaled, schematic representation of a process that shows equipment, interconnecting process lines, flow streams, operating conditions, and control instrumentation using standardized symbols and conventions.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Blueprint"

Imagine you are baking a cake. Before you start, you would write a *recipe* — a list of ingredients (inputs), the sequence of steps (mixing, baking, frosting), the utensils (mixer, oven), and the final outcome (cake). 

A **Flow Diagram is the industrial-scale recipe blueprint** of a chemical plant:

| Baking Analogy | Process Engineering Equivalent |
|----------------|-------------------------------|
| Recipe list | **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** |
| Step-by-step cooking procedure | **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** |
| Detailed oven temperature controls, timers, alarms | **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** |
| Ingredient amounts in grams | Material balance (kg/h) |
| Oven temperature & time | Operating conditions (°C, kPa) |

Just as a master chef cannot open a restaurant without a tested recipe, a chemical engineer cannot commission a plant without properly engineered flow diagrams.

> [!NOTE]
> The three-tier hierarchy — **BFD → PFD → P&ID** — is universally accepted across EPC (Engineering, Procurement, and Construction) industries such as petroleum refining, petrochemicals, pharmaceuticals, and food processing. KTU examiners consistently test this hierarchy.

## 1.3 Why Flow Diagrams Matter in KTU Project Engineering

- They serve as the **single source of truth** for multidisciplinary design (process, mechanical, instrumentation, civil, electrical).
- They enable **safety analyses** such as HAZOP and HAZID.
- They form the **legal and contractual basis** for equipment procurement and construction tenders.
- They are indispensable for **as-built documentation** during plant commissioning and operations.

---

> **Standard Reference Metric:** ISA (International Society of Automation) **S5.1** is the global benchmark for instrumentation symbols and identification used on P&IDs. Most **80%** of KTU questions on flow diagrams reference this convention.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Hierarchy of Process Flow Documentation

Process engineering documentation evolves in increasing detail as a project moves through its lifecycle. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly names three canonical tiers.

### 2.1.1 Block Flow Diagram (BFD) — The Strategic View

The **BFD** is the simplest, most zoomed-out representation. It depicts the overall process as a series of **rectangular blocks**, each representing a major process section (e.g., *Reactor*, *Distillation*, *Compressor*), connected by straight lines representing process streams.

- **Why it exists:** To give management, clients, and licensing authorities a one-page overview of material flow.
- **How it is built:** Begin with raw-material input → major transformation blocks → product output → recycle loops.
- **Information density:** Very low. Only stream identities (e.g., *Feed*, *Product*, *Recycle*) are shown — no flow rates, no equipment sizes.

### 2.1.2 Process Flow Diagram (PFD) — The Engineering View

The **PFD** is the workhorse of process design. It contains every major piece of equipment drawn to approximate scale, every process line, every utility stream, and tabular data blocks (stream tables) giving composition, flow rate, temperature, pressure, and phase for each numbered stream.

- **Why it exists:** To be the basis for **process equipment design, capital cost estimation (±30% accuracy)**, and preliminary hazard analysis.
- **How it is built:** BFD is expanded. Each block becomes a pictorial representation (cylindrical reactors, distillation columns with trays, pumps, compressors, heat exchangers). Process lines are numbered. A **stream table** is attached.
- **Information density:** High — but **instrumentation and control loops are deliberately omitted** (that is the job of the P&ID).

### 2.1.3 Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) — The Construction View

The **P&ID** is the most detailed, the most legalistic, and the most labor-intensive of the three. Every pipe, every valve, every instrument, every control loop, every line number, every insulation spec is shown. It is the **master document for plant construction and operator training**.

- **Why it exists:** To instruct pipefitters, instrument technicians, and operators on exactly what to build and how to control it.
- **How it is built:** PFD is the starting canvas. All instrumentation (sensors, controllers, valves) is overlaid following ISA-5.1 symbology. Line numbers, pipe specs, valve tags are added.
- **Information density:** Extreme. A single P&ID of a refinery unit may contain 1,000+ tagged items.

## 2.2 The Logic Steps to Construct a Flow Diagram

1. **Define process objective and chemistry:** Identify feed, desired product, by-products, reaction stoichiometry.
2. **Sketch the block flow:** Draw the major sections as rectangles with directional arrows.
3. **Select unit operations:** Decide reactors, separators, heat exchangers, pumps.
4. **Perform material and energy balance:** Calculate stream-by-stream flow rates (kg/h), compositions, temperatures, pressures.
5. **Convert blocks to equipment symbols:** Use KTU- and ISPE-recommended pictorial symbols.
6. **Number every process line:** Each unique stream gets a unique number.
7. **Add stream table:** Tabulate stream properties.
8. **Overlay instrumentation (P&ID stage):** Add ISA symbols for measurement, control, and safety devices.
9. **Review and revise:** Iterate with process safety, operations, and maintenance teams.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although flow diagrams are graphical, the engineer must perform **quantitative calculations** to populate them. The following table summarizes the high-yield equations:

| # | Concept | Equation | Symbols & Units | KTU Application |
|---|---------|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Overall Mass Balance | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dfrac{dM}{dt}$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/h), $M$ = holdup (kg) | Steady state: $dM/dt = 0$ |
| 2 | Component Mass Balance | $\sum \dot{m}_{i,\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{i,\text{out}} + \dot{r}_{i,\text{gen}}$ | $\dot{r}_{i,\text{gen}}$ = rate of generation (kg/h) | Tracks each species through reactors |
| 3 | Conversion (Single Pass) | $X = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_{A,\text{out}}}{F_{A0}}$ | $F_A$ = molar flow of reactant A (kmol/h) | Reactor performance |
| 4 | Overall Conversion (with Recycle) | $X_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{F_{A0,\text{fresh}} - F_{A,\text{out}}}{F_{A0,\text{fresh}}}$ | Same as above | Distinguishes from single-pass |
| 5 | Recycle Ratio | $R = \dfrac{F_{\text{recycle}}}{F_{\text{fresh feed}}}$ | Dimensionless | Economics of unreacted feed |
| 6 | Yield | $Y = \dfrac{\text{moles of desired product formed}}{\text{moles of limiting reactant fed}}$ | Dimensionless (or %) | Process selection |
| 7 | Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{\text{desired product formed}}{\text{total product formed}}$ | Dimensionless (or %) | Competing-reaction networks |
| 8 | Energy Balance (Reactor) | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} h_{\text{in}} + Q = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} h_{\text{out}}$ | $h$ = specific enthalpy (kJ/kg), $Q$ = heat duty (kW) | Heat exchanger sizing |
| 9 | Molar Flow Conversion | $\dot{n}_i = \dfrac{\dot{m}_i}{MW_i}$ | $\dot{n}$ = molar flow (kmol/h), $MW$ = molecular weight (kg/kmol) | Converting mass↔molar basis |
| 10 | Material Balance for a Mixer | $\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3$ | (kg/h) | Stream combining node |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `pipe` ($\vert$) character has been deliberately replaced by `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions to prevent breaking the markdown table. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if your equations are syntactically broken.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Flow Diagrams

- **Refining & Petrochemicals:** Designing crude distillation, catalytic cracking, hydrotreating units.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Validating cGMP-compliant batch and continuous processes.
- **Food & Beverages:** Designing pasteurization, fermentation, and packaging lines.
- **Water Treatment:** Schematic for coagulation, filtration, and reverse-osmosis trains.
- **Power Generation:** Steam cycle flow diagrams for thermal and nuclear plants.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

Because flow diagrams are inherently graphical, the *derivation* aspect for this KTU topic is best presented as a **step-by-step analytical walkthrough** of how a BFD is progressively expanded into a PFD and then a P&ID, with worked numerical material balance problems along the way.

## 3.1 Worked Example — A Simple Mixer-Heater System

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A chemical plant mixes two streams: Stream 1 (Benzene) at 500 kg/h, 25 °C and Stream 2 (Toluene) at 300 kg/h, 25 °C. The combined stream is heated in a heat exchanger to 80 °C before entering a reactor. *Draw the BFD and PFD, and perform the material and energy balance.*

### Step 1 — Draw the Block Flow Diagram (BFD)

Three major blocks are identified:
- **Mixer (M-101)** — combines streams 1 and 2.
- **Heater (E-101)** — raises the mixed stream temperature.
- **Reactor (R-101)** — performs the chemical reaction (here assumed isothermal; not detailed).

### Step 2 — Perform Overall Mass Balance on the Mixer

At steady state, the mixer obeys conservation of mass:

$$
\dot{m}_{1} + \dot{m}_{2} = \dot{m}_{3}
$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$
500 \;\text{kg/h} + 300 \;\text{kg/h} = \dot{m}_{3}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{3} = 800 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

Therefore, the **combined stream leaving the mixer (Stream 3) is 800 kg/h**.

### Step 3 — Compute the Composition of Stream 3

The mass fraction of benzene in Stream 3 is:

$$
x_{\text{benzene}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{1}}{\dot{m}_{3}} = \dfrac{500}{800} = 0.625
$$

The mass fraction of toluene is:

$$
x_{\text{toluene}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{2}}{\dot{m}_{3}} = \dfrac{300}{800} = 0.375
$$

### Step 4 — Perform the Energy Balance on the Heater

Use the steady-state energy balance (neglecting kinetic and potential energy, and assuming no phase change):

$$
\dot{m}_{3} \cdot C_{p,\text{mix}} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) = Q
$$

Assuming the mixture's specific heat capacity is the mass-weighted average:

$$
C_{p,\text{benzene}} = 1.74 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

$$
C_{p,\text{toluene}} = 1.70 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

$$
C_{p,\text{mix}} = (0.625)(1.74) + (0.375)(1.70) = 1.0875 + 0.6375 = 1.725 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

Now compute the temperature rise:

$$
\Delta T = 80 - 25 = 55 \;^{\circ}\text{C} = 55 \;\text{K}
$$

Therefore the heat duty required is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_{3} \cdot C_{p,\text{mix}} \cdot \Delta T \\
  &= 800 \;\text{kg/h} \times 1.725 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)} \times 55 \;\text{K} \\
  &= 75{,}900 \;\text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to kW (divide by 3600):

$$
Q = \dfrac{75{,}900}{3600} = 21.08 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Step 5 — Tabulate the Stream Information (Stream Table)

| Stream No. | From → To | Phase | Flow (kg/h) | T (°C) | P (kPa) | Composition (mass) |
|:----------:|:---------:|:-----:|:-----------:|:------:|:-------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | Feed 1 → M-101 | Liquid | 500 | 25 | 200 | Benzene 1.00 |
| 2 | Feed 2 → M-101 | Liquid | 300 | 25 | 200 | Toluene 1.00 |
| 3 | M-101 → E-101 | Liquid | 800 | 25 | 200 | Benzene 0.625, Toluene 0.375 |
| 4 | E-101 → R-101 | Liquid | 800 | 80 | 195 | Benzene 0.625, Toluene 0.375 |

### Step 6 — Identify What a P&ID Would Add

The P&ID built on top of the PFD would specify:
- A **temperature transmitter (TT-101)** on Stream 4.
- A **temperature controller (TIC-101)** that modulates a control valve (TV-101) on the steam line entering E-101.
- A **flow indicator (FI-101)** on Stream 3.
- A **pressure safety valve (PSV-101)** on the reactor.
- All pipe sizes (e.g., 2″ Sch 40 CS), line numbers, and material specifications.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing BFD, PFD, and P&ID

| Feature | BFD | PFD | P&ID |
|---------|-----|-----|------|
| Purpose | Conceptual overview | Engineering basis | Construction & operations |
| Equipment representation | Rectangular blocks | Pictorial symbols | Pictorial with full tags |
| Stream data | Names only | Full stream table | Line-by-line specs |
| Instrumentation | None | None (or very minimal) | Complete ISA-5.1 symbols |
| Used for | Feasibility study | PFD becomes P&ID basis | Procurement & construction |
| Approximate cost-estimate accuracy | ±50% | ±30% | ±10% |
| Typical page size of a unit | 1 page | 1–3 pages | 5–20 pages |

## 4.5 Equipment Symbol Reference (KTU-Approved Pictograms)

| Equipment | Standard Symbol Description | Common Notation on Diagram |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Centrifugal pump | Circle with impeller and suction/discharge nozzles | P-101 A or B |
| Shell-and-tube heat exchanger | Cylindrical shell with internal tube bundle | E-101 |
| Distillation column | Tall vertical cylinder with trays and reboiler/condenser | T-101 |
| Horizontal vessel / drum | Horizontal cylinder with saddle supports | V-101 |
| Compressor | Circle with double impeller symbol | C-101 |
| Control valve | Bow-tie or globe valve symbol on a process line | XV-101 |
| Safety relief valve | Spring-loaded symbol on top of a vessel | PSV-101 |
| Reactor (CSTR) | Cylindrical vessel with agitator, jacket | R-101 |
