# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Module 1]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, explain the purpose and key features of a Block Flow Diagram (BFD) in process engineering. Mention its limitations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Split: 1 + 1 + 1):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A Block Flow Diagram is a simplified representation of an overall process in which each major process section is shown as a rectangular block, with process streams connecting them as arrows.
- **Key Features (1 mark):** It provides a one-page process overview, identifies the major transformation steps, shows feed and product flow direction, and is used for feasibility studies and management communication.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** It does not show equipment details, material/energy balances, operating conditions, instrumentation, or utility requirements; hence it cannot be used for equipment design or construction.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**
*Differentiate between a Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and a Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). Mention any four distinct features.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.75 each, 4 points):**

| Sl. | PFD | P&ID |
|-----|-----|------|
| 1 | Shows major equipment and stream data | Shows every pipe, valve, and instrument |
| 2 | No or minimal instrumentation | Complete ISA-5.1 instrumentation symbols |
| 3 | Used for process design basis | Used for construction and operations |
| 4 | Includes material and energy balance tables | Includes line numbers, pipe specs, valve tags |
| 5 | Does not show control loops | Shows all control and interlock loops |

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*(a)* Explain, with neat sketches, the three-tier hierarchy of process flow diagrams used in chemical plant design. Discuss the level of information provided at each tier and the stage of the project at which it is prepared. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A process stream consisting of 1,200 kg/h of a 40% (by mass) aqueous NaOH solution is mixed with a recycle stream of 800 kg/h containing 20% NaOH. The combined stream is sent to an evaporator. Determine the mass flow rate, the composition, and the mass flow of pure NaOH in the combined stream. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Three-Tier Hierarchy (7 marks)

- **Tier 1 — Block Flow Diagram (BFD):** Created during the *conceptual / feasibility* stage (1 mark). Shows major process sections as blocks. Limited information: only stream names (feed, product, recycle). No balances, no equipment detail (1 mark).
- **Tier 2 — Process Flow Diagram (PFD):** Created during the *basic engineering / FEED* stage (1 mark). Shows all major equipment pictorially, all numbered streams, and a full stream table with composition, T, P, phase, flow rate (1 mark). Used for equipment sizing, mass/energy balance, and ±30% cost estimate.
- **Tier 3 — Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID):** Created during the *detailed engineering* stage (1 mark). Built upon the PFD by adding instrumentation per ISA-5.1, all valves (manual, control, safety), line numbers, pipe specs, insulation, and control-loop description (1 mark). Used as the legal construction document and for HAZOP, commissioning, and operations (1 mark).

#### Part (b) — Mass Balance on the Mixer (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Fresh stream: $\dot{m}_1 = 1200$ kg/h, $w_{\text{NaOH},1} = 0.40$
- Recycle stream: $\dot{m}_2 = 800$ kg/h, $w_{\text{NaOH},2} = 0.20$

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = 1200 + 800 = 2000 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Pure NaOH in Each Stream (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{NaOH},1} = 1200 \times 0.40 = 480 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{NaOH},2} = 800 \times 0.20 = 160 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Total NaOH in Combined Stream (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{NaOH},3} = 480 + 160 = 640 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Composition of Combined Stream (2 marks):**

$$
w_{\text{NaOH},3} = \dfrac{640}{2000} = 0.32 \quad \text{i.e., 32\% NaOH by mass}
$$

**Step 5 — Tabulated Answer (1 mark):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Total mass flow of Stream 3 | **2000 kg/h** |
| Mass fraction NaOH in Stream 3 | **0.32 (32%)** |
| Mass flow of pure NaOH in Stream 3 | **640 kg/h** |

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam]**
*(a)* Draw a labelled Process Flow Diagram (PFD) for a process in which 1,000 kg/h of a feed (Component A, 50% by mass) is mixed with a recycle stream, heated in a heat exchanger from 30 °C to 90 °C, sent to a reactor where 80% of A is converted per pass, and the reactor effluent is separated into a product stream and a recycle stream. Show the stream table for at least four streams. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* For the same process, calculate: (i) the conversion per pass, (ii) the recycle ratio, and (iii) the heat duty of the heat exchanger, given $C_{p,\text{mix}} = 2.1$ kJ/(kg·K). Assume steady state. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — PFD Sketch and Stream Table (7 marks)

A neat PFD should show: Mixer → Heater → Reactor → Separator → (Product out + Recycle back to Mixer). Stream table (any four streams) — full marks if all numbered arrows, equipment pictograms, and a stream table with composition, T, P, and flow are present. (1 mark for layout, 1 mark for each of: stream numbers, equipment symbols, stream table completeness — up to 6 marks; 1 mark for neatness and labelling).

**Sample Stream Table (1 mark for completion):**

| Stream | From → To | T (°C) | P (kPa) | Mass Flow (kg/h) | Component A Mass Fraction |
|:------:|:---------:|:------:|:-------:|:----------------:|:-------------------------:|
| 1 | Fresh Feed → Mixer | 30 | 200 | 1000 | 0.50 |
| 2 | Recycle → Mixer | 35 | 200 | 500 | 0.30 |
| 3 | Mixer → Heater | 31 | 200 | 1500 | 0.433 |
| 4 | Heater → Reactor | 90 | 195 | 1500 | 0.433 |

#### Part (b) — Quantitative Calculations (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Conversion per pass (1 mark):**

$$
X_{\text{pass}} = \dfrac{F_{A,\text{in,reactor}} - F_{A,\text{out,reactor}}}{F_{A,\text{in,reactor}}} = 0.80 \;\text{(given)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the fresh feed and recycle composition (1 mark):**

Mass of A in Stream 1: $1000 \times 0.50 = 500$ kg/h.  
Mass of A in Stream 2: $500 \times 0.30 = 150$ kg/h.  
Mass of A in Stream 3: $500 + 150 = 650$ kg/h.

**Step 3 — Single-pass A leaving the reactor (1 mark):**

$$
F_{A,\text{out,reactor}} = 650 \times (1 - 0.80) = 130 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Recycle Ratio (1 mark):**

$$
R = \dfrac{F_{\text{recycle}}}{F_{\text{fresh feed}}} = \dfrac{500}{1000} = 0.5
$$

**Step 5 — Heat Duty (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_{\text{combined}} \cdot C_{p,\text{mix}} \cdot \Delta T \\
  &= 1500 \;\text{kg/h} \times 2.1 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)} \times (90 - 30) \;\text{K} \\
  &= 1500 \times 2.1 \times 60 \;\text{kJ/h} \\
  &= 189{,}000 \;\text{kJ/h} \\
  &= \dfrac{189{,}000}{3600} = 52.5 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**  
(i) Conversion per pass = **0.80 (80%)**  
(ii) Recycle ratio = **0.5**  
(iii) Heat duty of the heater = **52.5 kW**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Students often forget to convert kJ/h to kW (divide by 3600). The KTU model answer key explicitly checks this. Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Stream numbering trap:** Numbering must be unique per stream; the same line cannot have two numbers. The examiner will deduct 0.5 mark per duplicate.
> 3. **Phase-omission trap:** Stream tables must specify phase (V/L/V+L) for every stream. A common error is omitting it for the reactor effluent.
> 4. **Recycle vs single-pass conversion trap:** Students confuse overall conversion with single-pass conversion. Always state which one you are computing.
> 5. **P&ID vs PFD trap:** If the question asks for a PFD, do not waste 10 minutes drawing instrumentation. If it asks for a P&ID, do not submit a PFD — the examiner will mark zero for instrumentation.
> 6. **Basis trap:** Always state the basis (e.g., "Basis: 1000 kg/h of fresh feed") before starting the calculation. Examiners reward this discipline with 0.5 bonus mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition anchor:** Process engineering transforms laboratory chemistry into plant-scale operating reality; flow diagrams are its primary communication tool.
- **Three-tier hierarchy:** **BFD → PFD → P&ID** (concept → engineering basis → construction).
- **BFD shows:** blocks + stream names only.
- **PFD shows:** pictorial equipment, all stream numbers, full stream table (composition, flow, T, P, phase), no detailed instrumentation.
- **P&ID shows:** everything — every pipe, valve, instrument, control loop, line spec, following **ISA-5.1** symbols.
- **Core equations to memorize:** Overall mass balance, component mass balance, conversion, recycle ratio, yield, selectivity, energy balance, and molar flow conversion.
- **Symbols to recall:** Mixer (circle with arrow), pump (circle with impeller), heat exchanger (cylinder with bundle), distillation column (tall cylinder with trays), reactor (jacketed vessel with agitator), control valve (bow-tie), safety valve (spring on vessel).
- **Stream table columns to remember:** Stream No. → From → To → Phase → Flow → T → P → Composition.
- **ISA tag format:** First letter = measured variable (T, P, F, L); subsequent letters = function (I, C, V, A, S); suffix number = loop number (e.g., **TIC-101** = Temperature Indicating Controller, Loop 101).
- **Always declare your basis** at the start of any numerical question.
- **Always check units** for consistency (kg/h, kJ/h vs kW, K vs °C).
- **The pipe character** in LaTeX expressions must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` — never the raw `|` — to keep markdown tables intact.
- **The hierarchy maps to project stages:** Feasibility → Basic Engineering (FEED) → Detailed Engineering → Construction → Commissioning → Operations.
- **Project Engineering integration:** Flow diagrams are the *lingua franca* between process, mechanical, instrumentation, civil, electrical, safety, and procurement teams — mastering them is essential for any project engineer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineering Design and Drafting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT631 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineering Design and Drafting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Design** is the systematic, iterative process of applying scientific principles, mathematical models, engineering experience, and creative judgment to produce a functional, economical, and manufacturable solution to a clearly defined technical problem or need. It is the intellectual heart of all engineering disciplines.

**Engineering Drafting (Engineering Drawing)** is the universally standardized graphical language used by engineers and technologists to communicate geometric, dimensional, and procedural information about engineered artifacts. It serves as the **primary medium of technical communication** between designer, manufacturer, inspector, and end user.

According to the **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** — adopting **ISO 128** — engineering drawings follow a strict set of conventions for projection, line work, dimensioning, and tolerancing so that any qualified engineer, anywhere in the world, can interpret them unambiguously.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In PEPOT631 (Project Engineering), Module 1 establishes the *lingua franca* of engineering. Without a working knowledge of design philosophy and drafting conventions, no project can be planned, costed, fabricated, or certified.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building a House" Intuition

Think of **engineering design** as the act of *imagining and planning* a house in your head: deciding how many rooms it should have, where the kitchen should face, how sunlight enters, what the budget allows.

**Engineering drafting**, in this analogy, is the *blueprint* — the paper (or digital file) that you hand over to the contractor so that what is inside your head becomes a buildable, verifiable plan.

| Stage in House Analogy | Equivalent in Engineering |
|---|---|
| Recognizing need (no roof, rain comes in) | Problem Identification |
| Sketching a rough floor plan | Conceptual Design |
| Hiring an architect to refine plans | Preliminary Design |
| Final detailed plan with measurements | Detailed Design |
| Blueprint handed to construction team | Engineering Drawing / Drafting |
| Construction begins | Manufacturing / Fabrication |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** A *design* exists in the mind as a concept. A *drawing* exists on paper (or screen) as the *legal, technical contract* between designer and maker. No design is "real" until it is drafted to the correct standard.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Engineering Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every engineering design MUST balance three concurrent pillars — violation of any one collapses the project.

1. **Function** — Does it do what it must?
2. **Form** — Is it geometrically sound and aesthetically acceptable?
3. **Economy** — Can it be built within time, cost, and resource constraints?

**Physical Constants & Standards referenced throughout this module:**

- **Standard Sheet Sizes (BIS/ISO):** A0 (1189 × 841 mm), A1, A2, A3, A4
- **Drawing Scales (typical):** Enlargement — **2:1, 5:1**; Reduction — **1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100**
- **Type of Lines (ISO 128):** Continuous thick (visible edges), continuous thin (dimensions), dashed (hidden edges), chain (centerlines), chain thick (cutting planes)
- **Tolerance Grade fundamentals:** IT01 → IT18 (IT = International Tolerance grade per **ISO 286**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engineering Design

Design problems are categorized by their novelty and the maturity of the solution space. The classification directly dictates the **risk profile**, **time investment**, and **innovation tax** of a project.

### Type 1 — Adaptive (Variant) Design
- Resolving a **minor conflict** in an existing design.
- Most common in industry (~**80%** of all design activity).
- **Analogy:** Updating a smartphone's camera module without altering the body.

### Type 2 — Developmental (Re-design) Design
- Substantial modification of an existing product for **better performance, lower cost, or new materials**.
- **Analogy:** Converting a petrol car to a hybrid — same purpose, new subsystem.

### Type 3 — New (Original) Design
- Inventing a **novel solution** to a problem never solved before.
- Highest risk, longest gestation, requires R&D.
- **Analogy:** Designing the first reusable rocket booster.

---

## 2.2 The Engineering Design Process (Iterative)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The design process is **never linear**. It is a closed loop where each iteration refines the solution until all constraints are simultaneously satisfied.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Recognition of Need** — Market gap, customer complaint, regulatory push.
2. **Problem Definition** — Write a precise *design brief* with inputs, outputs, constraints.
3. **Concept Generation** — Brainstorming, TRIZ, morphological charts, biomimicry.
4. **Concept Selection** — Use a **decision matrix** (Pugh Matrix, Weighted Scoring) to filter concepts.
5. **Preliminary Design** — Block diagrams, schematic layouts, rough calculations, feasibility check.
6. **Detailed Design** — Final dimensions, tolerances, material grades, surface finish codes.
7. **Prototyping & Testing** — Physical or virtual (FEA, CFD) validation.
8. **Iteration** — Return to Step 3 if tests fail; refine until acceptable.
9. **Production Documentation** — Release of final drawings, BOMs, and inspection checklists.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Drafting — Categories of Drawings

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 PEPOT631 syllabus specifically tests your ability to **identify which drawing type fits which project phase**.

| Category | Purpose | Produced At |
|---|---|---|
| **Concept / Freehand Sketch** | Rapid idea capture, design reviews | Concept Generation |
| **Layout Drawing** | Overall arrangement, no detail | Preliminary Design |
| **Assembly Drawing** | Shows how parts fit together | Detailed Design |
| **Detail Drawing** | Manufacturing info for ONE part | Detailed Design |
| **Schematic Diagram** | Functional flow (P&ID, circuit) | System Engineering |
| **Installation Drawing** | Site erection information | Project Execution |
| **As-Built Drawing** | Reflects actual final state | Project Closure |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Projection Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners love asking:** "Which projection method is used in First Angle vs Third Angle?" This is the single most-asked Module 1 question.

| Parameter | First Angle Projection | Third Angle Projection |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard followed in** | **ISO** (Europe, India, China) | **ASME / ANSI** (USA, Canada) |
| **Object placement** | Between observer and projection plane | Projection plane between observer and object |
| **Front view (FV)** | Center | Center |
| **Top View (TV)** | Placed **below** FV | Placed **above** FV |
| **Left Side View (LSV)** | Placed on the **right** of FV | Placed on the **left** of FV |
| **Symbol** | Truncated cone with FV smaller side facing cone | Truncated cone with FV larger side facing cone |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Drafting Conventions

### Line Conventions (as per BIS SP:46 & ISO 128)

| Line Type | Application |
|---|---|
| **Continuous thick** | Visible edges, outlines |
| **Continuous thin** | Dimension lines, hatching, leader lines |
| **Dashed thick** | Hidden edges (visible as hidden) |
| **Chain thin** | Centerlines, pitch circles |
| **Chain thick** at ends, thin elsewhere | Cutting plane lines |

### Dimensioning Rules (Four Pillars)

1. **Function first** — Dimensions required for function of the part go first.
2. **No double dimensioning** — Never repeat the same length.
3. **Avoid crossing leaders** — Keep dimension lines short and readable.
4. **Aligned vs Unidirectional** — Aligned (parallel to dimension line) is BIS default; Unidirectional (horizontal text) is ASME default.

### Limits, Fits & Tolerances — KTU Formula

For any dimension:

$$\text{Tolerance} = \text{Upper Limit (UL)} - \text{Lower Limit (LL)}$$

$$\text{Mean Dimension} = \frac{\text{UL} + \text{LL}}{2}$$

$$\text{Allowance} = \text{Maximum clearance (for clearance fit)}$$

$$\text{Tolerance Grade (IT)} = \text{Fundamental Tolerance} = f(\text{Basic Size, IT grade})$$

The fundamental tolerance (in micrometres, $\mu m$) for grades IT5 to IT18 is computed as:

$$T = a \cdot i$$

where $i$ (the tolerance unit in $\mu m$) is:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D$$

with $D$ being the geometric mean of the size range in millimetres, and $a$ being a grade-specific multiplier.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Fits and tolerances determine whether a shaft will *press-fit, slide-fit, or slip-fit* into a hole. This single decision influences cost, assembly time, lubrication, and product lifetime.

---

## 2.6 Modern Drafting — From Manual to CAD/CAM

| Aspect | Manual Drafting | Computer-Aided Design (CAD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tools** | T-square, set-squares, compass, drafter | Software: AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, NX |
| **Revision** | Redo entire sheet | Parametric update in seconds |
| **Storage** | Physical drawers | Cloud-based PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) |
| **Accuracy** | Limited by hand precision | Up to **0.0001 mm** precision |
| **3D Capability** | None (or isometric sketches) | Full solid, surface, mesh modelling |
| **Parametric** | Not possible | Fully supported — change one dimension, whole model updates |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reality Check:** While industry has moved to CAD/PLM, KTU 2024 PEPOT631 still expects students to read and produce **manual orthographic projections**, as they form the *conceptual foundation* for understanding 3D CAD operations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although "Engineering Design and Drafting" is largely conceptual, **KTU examiners expect quantitative problem-solving** on dimensioning, tolerances, scales, and projection-based geometry. Below are the three highest-yield analytical patterns.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Pattern A — Tolerance Stack-Up

### Problem Statement
A shaft of basic diameter **50 mm** is to fit into a hole. The hole tolerance is given as **+0.025 / 0.000 mm** and the shaft tolerance is given as **−0.025 / −0.050 mm**. Determine:
1. Type of fit
2. Maximum clearance
3. Minimum clearance

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the basic size and limits.

$$\text{Basic Size} = 50.000 \text{ mm}$$

Hole limits:

$$H_{\max} = 50.000 + 0.025 = 50.025 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{\min} = 50.000 + 0.000 = 50.000 \text{ mm}$$

Shaft limits:

$$S_{\max} = 50.000 - 0.025 = 49.975 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_{\min} = 50.000 - 0.050 = 49.950 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute Maximum Clearance.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} &= H_{\max} - S_{\min} \\
C_{\max} &= 50.025 - 49.950 \\
C_{\max} &= +0.075 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Minimum Clearance.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\min} &= H_{\min} - S_{\max} \\
C_{\min} &= 50.000 - 49.975 \\
C_{\min} &= +0.025 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the fit type.

$$\text{Since } C_{\min} > 0 \text{ and } C_{\max} > 0 \implies \text{CLEARANCE FIT}$$

**Step 5:** Compute mean clearance.

$$\bar{C} = \frac{C_{\max} + C_{\min}}{2} = \frac{0.075 + 0.025}{2} = 0.050 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Pattern B — Drawing Scale Conversion

### Problem Statement
A line in a drawing measures **72 mm**. The drawing scale is **1:5**. Determine:
1. The actual (true) length of the object.
2. The line length on a reduced drawing at **1:20**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert the scale to ratio form.

$$\text{Scale} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

**Step 2:** Compute the true (actual) length.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{actual}} &= L_{\text{drawing}} \times \text{Denominator} \\
L_{\text{actual}} &= 72 \text{ mm} \times 5 \\
L_{\text{actual}} &= 360 \text{ mm} = 0.36 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the new line length at 1:20 scale.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{new}} &= L_{\text{actual}} \times \frac{1}{20} \\
L_{\text{new}} &= 360 \times \frac{1}{20} \\
L_{\text{new}} &= 18 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Marks are awarded for stating the scale formula explicitly. Never write only the final number.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Pattern C — Orthographic Projection Conversion (True-Length by Rotation)

### Problem Statement
A line **AB** has projections: Top View length = **40 mm**, Front View length = **30 mm**, and it is inclined at **30°** to the HP in the top view. Find the true length and true inclinations.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** True length from two inclined projections.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{True Length (TL)} &= \sqrt{(\text{FV length})^2 + (\text{TV length})^2} \\
\text{TL} &= \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} \\
\text{TL} &= \sqrt{900 + 1600} \\
\text{TL} &= \sqrt{2500} \\
\text{TL} &= 50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** True inclination with HP ($\theta$).

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta &= \frac{\text{FV length}}{\text{TL}} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6 \\
\theta &= \arcsin(0.6) \approx 36.87^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** True inclination with VP ($\phi$).

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin\phi &= \frac{\text{TV length}}{\text{TL}} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.8 \\
\phi &= \arcsin(0.8) \approx 53.13^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Verification — angles must be greater than the apparent angles.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Check:} &\quad \theta = 36.87^\circ > 30^\circ \quad (\text{TV apparent inclination}) \\
&\quad \phi = 53.13^\circ > 0^\circ \quad (\text{parallel to VP in TV})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why angles increase when going from apparent to true:** When you rotate the line from its projected position back to its true 3D orientation, you are "unfolding" the projection — the 3D line is always longer than any single projection of itself.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Pattern D — Decision Matrix for Concept Selection

When multiple design concepts exist, the **Weighted Decision Matrix** quantifies selection. Steps:

1. List evaluation criteria (cost, weight, reliability, etc.)
2. Assign weight $w_i$ to each (sum = 1)
3. Score each concept $C_j$ on each criterion (1–10)
4. Compute weighted score: $S_j = \sum_i w_i \cdot C_{ij}$
5. Highest $S_j$ wins.

$$\text{Weighted Score } S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_{ij}$$

This is the **Pugh Matrix** (or Kepner-Tregoe) method used widely in DFSS (Design for Six Sigma).

---

## 3.5 Conceptual Mapping — Design vs Drafting Deliverables per Phase

| Project Phase | Design Output | Drafting Output |
|---|---|---|
| Concept | Idea, brief, sketches | Freehand sketch, conceptual block diagram |
| Feasibility | Functional specs, target metrics | Preliminary layout, schematic |
| Detailed | Final specs, BOM, FEA report | Assembly + Detail drawings |
| Production | Work instructions, QC plan | Shop drawings, jigs/fixtures drawing |
| Closure | Lessons learned | As-built drawing, O&M manual |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below use **strictly alphanumeric Mermaid node identifiers** (per the V10 anti-collision rules). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, etc.) are used as node names.

---

## 4.1 Engineering Design Process — Closed-Loop Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["Step 01 - Need Recognition"] --> n2["Step 02 - Problem Definition and Brief"]
    n2 --> n3["Step 03 - Concept Generation"]
    n3 --> n4["Step 04 - Concept Selection Matrix"]
    n4 --> n5["Step 05 - Preliminary Design"]
    n5 --> n6["Step 06 - Detailed Design"]
    n6 --> n7["Step 07 - Prototype and Testing"]
    n7 --> n8{"Test Pass?"}
    n8 -- No --> n9["Step 08 - Iterate and Refine"]
    n9 --> n3
    n8 -- Yes --> n10["Step 09 - Production Documentation"]
    n10 --> n11["Step 10 - Release to Manufacturing"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The decision node `n8` ("Test Pass?") is the pivot of the iterative loop. Failure sends the design back to Step 3, ensuring that no unvalidated concept reaches manufacturing.

---

## 4.2 Engineering Drafting Hierarchy — From Idea to Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Freehand Sketch"] --> a2["Layout Drawing"]
    a2 --> a3["Assembly Drawing"]
    a3 --> a4["Detail Drawing A"]
    a3 --> a5["Detail Drawing B"]
    a3 --> a6["Detail Drawing C"]
    a4 --> a7["BOM and Inspection Plan"]
    a5 --> a7
    a6 --> a7
    a7 --> a8["Shop Floor Release"]
```

**Interpretation:** One assembly drawing spawns multiple detail drawings (one per unique part). All detail drawings consolidate into a single BOM (Bill of Materials) that drives procurement and inventory.

---

## 4.3 First Angle vs Third Angle Projection — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph blockISO["FIRST ANGLE - ISO STANDARD - INDIA AND EU"]
      isoFV["Front View - Center"]
      isoTV["Top View - BELOW Front View"]
      isoLSV["Left Side View - RIGHT of Front View"]
      isoRS["Right Side View - LEFT of Front View"]
    end
    subgraph blockASME["THIRD ANGLE - ASME - USA AND CANADA"]
      asmFV["Front View - Center"]
      asmTV["Top View - ABOVE Front View"]
      asmLSV["Left Side View - LEFT of Front View"]
      asmRS["Right Side View - RIGHT of Front View"]
    end
```

**Critical Visual Cue:** In First Angle, the top view slides **downward** to unfold; in Third Angle, the top view rotates **upward**. Always confirm the standard before interpreting a drawing.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Design Office Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    b1["Customer or Market Need"] --> b2["Project Manager"]
    b2 --> b3["Design Lead"]
    b3 --> b4["Mechanical Design Team"]
    b3 --> b5["Electrical Design Team"]
    b3 --> b6["Software Design Team"]
    b4 --> b7["CAD Modelling Cell"]
    b5 --> b7
    b6 --> b7
    b7 --> b8["Design Review Board"]
    b8 --> b9{"Approved?"}
    b9 -- No --> b3
    b9 -- Yes --> b10["Document Control and Release"]
    b10 --> b11["Manufacturing and Supply Chain"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the **Design Review Board (DRB)** is the quality gate. In KTU PEPOT631 project contexts, every Phase Gate review mimics this exact pattern.

---

## 4.5 Tolerance Specification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    c1["Is mating part available?"] --> c2["Yes"]
    c1 --> c3["No"]
    c2 --> c4["Specify FITS - Clearance Transition or Interference"]
    c3 --> c5["Use General Tolerancing ISO 2768"]
    c4 --> c6["Calculate Limits using Fundamental Deviation"]
    c5 --> c7["Apply Medium Class m or Fine Class f"]
    c6 --> c8["Add Geometric Tolerances GD and T"]
    c7 --> c8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE for PEPOT631):** Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice. Module 1 contributes 20–25% of total marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Differentiate between Adaptive Design and Developmental Design with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Adaptive Design | Developmental Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Minor modification of an existing design to fix a specific issue | Major re-engineering of an existing product for improved performance |
| **Risk** | Low | Medium |
| **Innovation** | Minimal | Significant |
| **Example** | Changing fastener material from steel to SS in a pump housing to resist corrosion | Converting a manual lathe to a CNC lathe |

*(Marks: 1 definition + 1 example = 2 marks; comparison table = 1 mark)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**List any SIX important BIS/ISO conventions used in engineering drawings.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Line types** — Continuous thick, dashed, chain, etc. (ISO 128)
2. **Sheet sizes** — A0 to A4 (ISO 5457)
3. **Projection method** — First Angle (BIS) / Third Angle (ASME)
4. **Dimensioning rules** — Aligned placement, no duplication
5. **Scales** — Standard reduction/enlargement ratios
6. **Sectioning and hatching** — 45° parallel lines, spacing as per material

*(1 mark for each correct point = 3 marks for 6 points)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Full 14-Mark Question

**[Question A]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEPOT631]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **iterative engineering design process** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss any **FIVE** design considerations that influence the final outcome. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of basic diameter **80 mm** is to be assembled into a hub. The hole tolerance is **+0.030 / 0.000 mm** and the shaft tolerance is **−0.020 / −0.050 mm**. Determine: **(i)** Type of fit, **(ii)** Maximum clearance, **(iii)** Minimum clearance, **(iv)** Allowance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The engineering design process is an **iterative, closed-loop methodology** that transforms a problem statement into a fully validated solution.

**Stages (5 marks for explanation, 2 marks for diagram):**

1. **Need Recognition** — Identify the gap or problem.
2. **Problem Definition** — Draft the design brief with constraints.
3. **Concept Generation** — Brainstorm multiple alternatives using TRIZ, SCAMPER, etc.
4. **Concept Selection** — Apply Decision Matrix to rank concepts.
5. **Preliminary Design** — Functional block diagrams, rough calculations.
6. **Detailed Design** — Final dimensions, tolerances, materials.
7. **Prototype & Test** — Validate via FEA, physical prototype.
8. **Iteration Loop** — Return to Step 3 until validation passes.
9. **Documentation** — Release drawings, BOM, manuals.

**Five Design Considerations (each carry 1 mark, total 5 — already covered above; key examples):**

- **Functional** — Must meet performance specifications.
- **Economic** — Cost of materials, manufacturing, lifecycle.
- **Safety** — Factor of safety, failure mode analysis.
- **Ergonomic** — User comfort, accessibility.
- **Manufacturability** — Ease of fabrication, assembly, inspection.
- **Environmental** — Recyclability, sustainability, regulatory compliance.

*(Examiner note: any 5 are accepted; the answer above lists 6 to demonstrate coverage.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Given:
- Basic size = 80 mm
- Hole limits: $H_{\max} = 80.030 \text{ mm}$, $H_{\min} = 80.000 \text{ mm}$
- Shaft limits: $S_{\max} = 79.980 \text{ mm}$, $S_{\min} = 79.950 \text{ mm}$

**(i) Type of fit:**

$$C_{\min} = H_{\min} - S_{\max} = 80.000 - 79.980 = +0.020 \text{ mm}$$

Since $C_{\min} > 0$ and $C_{\max} > 0$, the fit is a **Clearance Fit (Sliding Fit)**. *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Maximum Clearance:**

$$C_{\max} = H_{\max} - S_{\min} = 80.030 - 79.950 = +0.080 \text{ mm}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Minimum Clearance:**

$$C_{\min} = 80.000 - 79.980 = +0.020 \text{ mm}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(iv) Allowance (Maximum Clearance for clearance fit):**

$$\text{Allowance} = C_{\max} = +0.080 \text{ mm}$$ *(1 mark)*

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

**[Question B]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEPOT631]`**

**(a)** What is **engineering drawing**? Explain the difference between **First Angle and Third Angle Projection** with neat sketches. State which standard is followed in India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drawing is prepared at a scale of **1:10**. A length of **150 mm** is drawn on the sheet. Later, the same part is to be redrawn on a **1:25** scale. Calculate: **(i)** Actual true length, **(ii)** Length on the new sheet. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Engineering Drawing Definition (2 marks):**

Engineering drawing is a standardized graphical language used to communicate precise geometric, dimensional, and procedural information about engineered objects. It acts as the **universal technical language** of engineers across disciplines and geographies.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | First Angle | Third Angle |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | ISO (India, EU) | ASME (USA) |
| **Front View** | Center | Center |
| **Top View Location** | Below FV | Above FV |
| **Left Side View** | Right of FV | Left of FV |
| **Object-Plane Position** | Between observer and plane | Plane between observer and object |

**Neat sketches** showing the placement of views must accompany the table.

**India follows: First Angle Projection as per BIS SP:46** *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Actual True Length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{true}} &= L_{\text{drawing}} \times \text{Denominator} \\
L_{\text{true}} &= 150 \text{ mm} \times 10 \\
L_{\text{true}} &= 1500 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$ *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Length on new sheet (1:25 scale):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{new}} &= L_{\text{true}} \times \frac{1}{25} \\
L_{\text{new}} &= 1500 \times \frac{1}{25} \\
L_{\text{new}} &= 60 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$ *(4 marks including substitution and final value)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Allowance with Tolerance.** Allowance is the *intentional* dimensional difference (positive for clearance). Tolerance is the *permissible variation* from the basic size.
> 2. **Mixing up First Angle and Third Angle view placements.** Most students place the top view *above* FV by reflex — this is wrong for BIS standards.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit.** Always write **mm** explicitly. A correct number without a unit is awarded partial credit only.
> 4. **Skipping the formula in numerical answers.** KTU examiners award 1 mark for the formula statement even if the final number is wrong. Never omit it.
> 5. **Submitting freehand sketches where neat diagrams are asked.** A freehand projection diagram will cost 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Writing "design" instead of "drafting" or vice versa.** These are distinct concepts — read the question stem carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision for PEPOT631 Module 1 — Engineering Design and Drafting:**

- [ ] Engineering Design = systematic, iterative, constraint-driven problem solving
- [ ] Three pillars: **Function, Form, Economy** — never compromise one
- [ ] Three types of design: **Adaptive, Developmental, New (Original)**
- [ ] Design process is a **closed loop** — iteration is mandatory, not optional
- [ ] Engineering Drafting = the **standardized graphical contract** between designer and maker
- [ ] BIS/ISO standards govern: **sheet sizes, line types, scales, projection method**
- [ ] **First Angle Projection** is the Indian (BIS) standard; **Third Angle** is the US (ASME) standard
- [ ] In First Angle: **Top View = BELOW FV; Left Side View = RIGHT of FV**
- [ ] **Tolerance** = UL − LL; **Mean Dimension** = (UL + LL) / 2
- [ ] **Allowance** = intentional clearance in a clearance fit
- [ ] Decision Matrix uses **weighted scoring**: $S_j = \sum_i w_i \cdot C_{ij}$
- [ ] **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing)** is the modern extension of classical tolerancing
- [ ] CAD tools (AutoCAD, SolidWorks, NX) are industry standard; manual drafting is the conceptual foundation
- [ ] Always state **formula → substitution → result** in numerical answers
- [ ] Draw **neat diagrams** with proper line conventions; freehand = loss of marks

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Engineering Design and Drafting | PEPOT631 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
