> ✅ **Final Answer (KTU Board Format):**
> **TCI ≈ ₹ 55.97 Lakhs (or ₹ 55,96,992)**

---

## 3.2 Hand's Method — Working Capital Derivation

**Hand's empirical rule** states that the working capital equals **4.5 months** of the total operating expenses (raw materials, labor, utilities, freight, maintenance), less **1 month of accounts payable**.

The governing equation is:

$$WC = \frac{4.5}{12} \times \left( RM + L + U + F + M \right) - \frac{1}{12} \times RM$$

where the symbols represent annual outlay on:
- $RM$ = Raw Materials
- $L$ = Labor
- $U$ = Utilities
- $F$ = Freight
- $M$ = Maintenance

### Numerical Worked Example — Hand's Method

**Problem Statement:**
*Estimate the working capital for a project with the following annual operating costs:*
- *Raw materials: ₹ 24,00,000*
- *Labor: ₹ 12,00,000*
- *Utilities: ₹ 8,00,000*
- *Freight: ₹ 4,00,000*
- *Maintenance: ₹ 4,00,000*

**Step 1 — Sum the Annual Operating Outlay**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= RM + L + U + F + M \\
&= 24,00,000 + 12,00,000 + 8,00,000 + 4,00,000 + 4,00,000 \\
&= 52,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 4.5/12 Multiplier**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC_{\text{gross}} &= \frac{4.5}{12} \times 52,00,000 \\
&= 0.375 \times 52,00,000 \\
&= 19,50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract 1/12 of Raw Materials (Accounts Payable Credit)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC_{\text{net}} &= 19,50,000 - \frac{1}{12} \times 24,00,000 \\
&= 19,50,000 - 2,00,000 \\
&= 17,50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** **Working Capital ≈ ₹ 17,50,000 (₹ 17.5 Lakhs)**

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Decision Matrix

| Estimation Goal | Available Data | Recommended Method | KTU Board Weightage |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Quick 1-week ballpark | Plant type only | **Lang Factor** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Preliminary FCI breakdown | Equipment list | **Module Factor (Hand)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| WC for greenfield project | Annual op-cost | **Hand's 4.5/12 rule** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| WC for established unit | Annual sales | **% of Sales method** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Refinery/petrochemical add-on | Major equipment costs | **OSBL factor + Lang** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Final project sanction | Full engineering | **Definitive estimate** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 3.4 Sensitivity — How Plant-Type Changes Lang Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Solid Processing:} \quad & f_{L} = 3.10 \\
\text{Fluid Processing:} \quad & f_{L} = 4.74 \\
\text{Mixed (Avg):} \quad & f_{L} = 3.63
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the difference?** Fluid processing requires **more piping, more instrumentation, more insulation, and more service facilities** (pumps, compressors) than solid processing, which is dominated by mechanical equipment (crushers, conveyors).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capital Requirement Hierarchy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Total Capital Investment] --> node2[Fixed Capital Investment]
    node1 --> node3[Working Capital]
    node1 --> node4[Startup Expenses]
    node2 --> node5[ISBL - Inside Battery Limits]
    node2 --> node6[OSBL - Outside Battery Limits]
    node5 --> node7[Purchased Equipment]
    node5 --> node8[Installation and Erection]
    node5 --> node9[Process Piping]
    node5 --> node10[Instrumentation and Control]
    node5 --> node11[Electrical Systems]
    node5 --> node12[Insulation and Painting]
    node5 --> node13[Process Buildings]
    node6 --> node14[Land and Yard]
    node6 --> node15[Service Facilities]
    node6 --> node16[Storage Tanks]
    node6 --> node17[Engineering and Supervision]
    node6 --> node18[Contingency Allowance]
    node3 --> node19[Raw Materials Inventory]
    node3 --> node20[Finished Goods Inventory]
    node3 --> node21[Accounts Receivable]
    node3 --> node22[Cash Reserves]
    node3 --> node23[Less Accounts Payable]
```

## 4.2 Capital Estimation — Tiered Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage0[Project Stage] --> stageA[Feasibility Stage]
    stage0 --> stageB[Scoping Stage]
    stage0 --> stageC[Engineering Stage]
    stageA --> method1[Order of Magnitude Estimate]
    method1 --> acc1[Accuracy minus 30% to plus 50%]
    method1 --> data1[Uses Past Plant Data]
    stageB --> method2[Study or Factored Estimate]
    method2 --> acc2[Accuracy plus minus 30%]
    method2 --> data2[Uses Lang Factor or Hand Method]
    stageC --> method3[Detailed or Definitive Estimate]
    method3 --> acc3[Accuracy plus minus 5 to 15%]
    method3 --> data3[Uses Full Engineering Documents]
    method2 --> subA[Lang Factor Method]
    method2 --> subB[Module Factor Method]
    method2 --> subC[OSBL Factor Method]
```

## 4.3 Working Capital — Component-Level Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    wc1[Working Capital NWC] --> wc2[Plus Raw Materials Inventory]
    wc1 --> wc3[Plus Finished Goods Inventory]
    wc1 --> wc4[Plus Accounts Receivable]
    wc1 --> wc5[Plus Cash Reserves]
    wc1 --> wc6[Less Accounts Payable]
    wc2 --> src1[Source Raw Material Cost]
    wc3 --> src2[Source Production Cost]
    wc4 --> src3[Source Credit Sales Policy]
    wc5 --> src4[Source Payroll and Utilities]
    wc6 --> src5[Source Supplier Credit Terms]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *Differentiate clearly between Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) and Working Capital (WC). List any two components of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Fixed Capital (FCI) | Working Capital (WC) |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| **Purpose** | Acquires permanent assets | Finances day-to-day operations |
| **Nature** | Long-term, illiquid | Short-term, liquid |
| **Recovery** | Depreciation-based | Recovered in operating cycle |
| **Examples** | Equipment, land, buildings | Raw materials, cash, receivables |

> **Key KTU Insight:** FCI is what you **spend once**; WC is what you **keep spending on**.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *State the Lang Factor equation. What are the typical Lang Factor values for (a) solid processing, (b) fluid processing, and (c) mixed plants?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Lang Factor equation is:

$$FCI = f_{L} \times C_{E}$$

where $C_{E}$ is the delivered equipment cost.

- (a) Solid processing: $f_{L} = 3.10$
- (b) Fluid processing: $f_{L} = 4.74$
- (c) Mixed plants: $f_{L} = 3.63$

> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention **"delivered cost"** to fetch full marks; do not write just "equipment cost."

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Working Capital Estimation using Hand's Method*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

> A chemical plant is to be commissioned with the following projected **annual operating costs**:
>
> | Cost Head | Annual Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
> | :-- | :-- |
> | Raw Materials | 240 |
> | Labor | 120 |
> | Utilities | 80 |
> | Freight & Distribution | 40 |
> | Maintenance | 40 |
>
> The total Fixed Capital Investment (FCI) is **₹ 12,00,000**, and the plant expects a 1-month credit from suppliers.
>
> **(a)** Estimate the **Working Capital** using Hand's empirical rule. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Verify the same using the **% of FCI method** (assuming working capital is 15% of FCI), and compare the two results. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a): Hand's Method (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Sum of annual operating costs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 240 + 120 + 80 + 40 + 40 = 520 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the 4.5/12 multiplier:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC_{\text{gross}} &= \frac{4.5}{12} \times 520 = 195 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract 1 month of raw material cost (Accounts Payable credit):

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC_{\text{net}} &= 195 - \frac{1}{12} \times 240 = 195 - 20 = 175 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Working Capital (Hand's Method) = ₹ 175 Lakhs**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b): % of FCI Method + Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Apply 15% of FCI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC_{\%} &= 0.15 \times 1200 = 180 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Comparison Table:

| Method | Working Capital (₹ Lakhs) | Basis |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Hand's Method | 175 | 4.5 months of operating cost |
| % of FCI | 180 | 15% of Fixed Capital |
| **Difference** | **5 (≈2.8%)** | Negligible — both valid |

> ✅ **Conclusion:** Both methods give closely matching estimates. For a **greenfield project with no historical data**, the % of FCI method is simpler; Hand's method is **more accurate** when operating cost data is detailed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Total Capital Investment using Lang Factor*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Pattern]**

> A new **solid-processing plant** is being planned. The following data is available:
>
> - FOB cost of major equipment: **₹ 20,00,000**
> - Freight & Insurance: **6% of FOB**
> - Taxes & Customs Duty: **10% of FOB**
> - Working Capital: **20% of FCI**
> - Start-up Expenses: **6% of FCI**
>
> **(a)** Compute the **Total Capital Investment** using the **Lang Factor method** (for solid processing). **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** If the same plant were reclassified as a **fluid-processing plant**, calculate the new TCI and comment on the % increase. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a): Solid Processing (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Delivered equipment cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{E} &= 20,00,000 + (0.06 \times 20,00,000) + (0.10 \times 20,00,000) \\
&= 20,00,000 + 1,20,000 + 2,00,000 \\
&= 23,20,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Lang Factor for solid processing ($f_L = 3.10$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
FCI &= 3.10 \times 23,20,000 = 71,92,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute WC and SUE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC &= 0.20 \times 71,92,000 = 14,38,400 \\
SUE &= 0.06 \times 71,92,000 = 4,31,520
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Total Capital Investment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCI &= 71,92,000 + 14,38,400 + 4,31,520 = 90,61,920
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **TCI (Solid) = ₹ 90.62 Lakhs**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b): Fluid Processing Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Apply Lang Factor for fluid processing ($f_L = 4.74$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
FCI_{\text{fluid}} &= 4.74 \times 23,20,000 = 1,09,96,800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute WC and SUE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC &= 0.20 \times 1,09,96,800 = 21,99,360 \\
SUE &= 0.06 \times 1,09,96,800 = 6,59,808
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total Capital Investment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCI_{\text{fluid}} &= 1,09,96,800 + 21,99,360 + 6,59,808 = 1,38,55,968
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Percentage Increase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Increase} &= \frac{1,38,55,968 - 90,61,920}{90,61,920} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{47,94,048}{90,61,920} \times 100 \\
&\approx 52.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **TCI (Fluid) = ₹ 138.56 Lakhs, which is ~52.9% higher than the solid-processing case.**

**Comment:** Fluid processing demands substantially higher capital due to **greater piping, instrumentation, insulation, and service-facility requirements** — confirming why **plant type is a critical input** in capital estimation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. ❌ **Forgetting the "Delivered Cost"** — Writing $f_L \times$ FOB instead of $f_L \times C_E$. Loses **1–2 marks** outright.
> 2. ❌ **Using the wrong Lang Factor** — Mixing up 3.10 vs 4.74. Always state the **plant type** before applying.
> 3. ❌ **Hand's Method — Skipping the AP deduction** — Writing only 4.5/12 × total without subtracting 1/12 × RM. Half-mark lost in part (a).
> 4. ❌ **Forgetting Start-up Expenses** in TCI — Many students compute only $FCI + WC$ instead of $FCI + WC + SUE$.
> 5. ❌ **Not mentioning units** in the final answer (₹ Lakhs vs ₹ Crores). Always annotate.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **TCI = FCI + WC + SUE** is the foundational equation — commit to memory.
- [x] **Fixed Capital** = permanent assets; **Working Capital** = operational float.
- [x] **ISBL** = process side; **OSBL** = utilities + offsites.
- [x] **Lang Factor ($f_L$):** Solid = 3.10, Fluid = 4.74, Mixed = 3.63.
- [x] **Hand's Rule for WC:** 4.5/12 of operating cost − 1/12 of RM.
- [x] **% of Sales WC:** 15%–25%; **% of FCI WC:** 10%–25%.
- [x] **OSBL factor** = 20%–50% of ISBL.
- [x] **Contingency** = 5%–10% of Direct Plant Cost.
- [x] **Estimation Tiers:** Order of Magnitude (-30%/+50%) → Factored (±30%) → Definitive (±5–15%).
- [x] Always compute **delivered equipment cost** before applying Lang Factor.
- [x] For greenfield projects, prefer **% of FCI**; for established units, prefer **% of Sales**.
- [x] Fluid plants cost **~50% more** than solid plants of identical equipment cost.

> 🏆 **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Board Exam:** In any numerical, write the **formula first**, then **substitute the values**, then **box the final answer with units**. This is exactly what KTU examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Production
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT631 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost of Production

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cost of Production

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Production (KTU 2024 Definition):** The total expenditure incurred by a manufacturing or project enterprise on the conversion of raw materials into finished goods or services, encompassing **Direct Material, Direct Labour, Direct Expenses, and a properly apportioned share of Overheads (Factory, Administrative, and Selling & Distribution).** It represents the aggregate monetary value of all resources consumed to bring a product or project deliverable to a usable state.

In KTU Project Engineering terminology, the *Cost of Production* is the foundation stone of every financial decision — from tender preparation to break-even analysis and profitability assessment. It is classified under the **Manufacturing/Project Account** in cost accounting frameworks.

## 1.2 The "Cake Factory" Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine a baker running a small cake factory:

- **Flour, sugar, eggs, butter** → these are *Direct Materials* (go entirely into the cake).
- **The baker's wages** → *Direct Labour* (specifically for cake-making).
- **Rent of the bakery, electricity, depreciation of the oven** → *Factory Overheads* (indirect, but essential).
- **Salary of the accountant, office rent** → *Administrative Overheads* (running the business, not the cake).
- **Cost of delivery vans, showrooms** → *Selling & Distribution Overheads* (getting the cake to the customer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Project Engineering:** A project manager must accurately estimate each of these layers *before* sanctioning a project. Underestimating overheads leads to cost overruns; overestimating leads to losing competitive bids. The KTU examiner expects you to know the **hierarchical structure** of cost buildup.

## 1.3 Standard Cost Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Prime Cost** = Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct/Chargeable Expenses
> - **Works Cost / Factory Cost** = Prime Cost + Factory Overheads
> - **Cost of Production** = Works Cost + Office & Administrative Overheads
> - **Total Cost / Cost of Sales** = Cost of Production + Selling & Distribution Overheads
> - **Selling Price** = Total Cost + Profit Margin

## 1.4 Classification of Costs — KTU Framework

Costs in a project environment are classified along **three primary axes**:

1. **By Nature (Element-wise):** Material, Labour, Expenses.
2. **By Function:** Manufacturing, Administrative, Selling, Research & Development.
3. **By Behaviour (Volume-wise):** Fixed, Variable, Semi-Variable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The emphasis is on **Cost Behaviour Classification** (Fixed vs Variable) and the **Element-wise Cost Buildup** to the final *Cost of Production* figure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Element-wise Cost Composition

The **Cost of Production** is built up additively in a strict hierarchical manner. Each layer absorbs the layer below it, plus its own specific overhead category.

**Step 1 — Direct Material (DM):**
The cost of all raw materials, components, and consumables that become physically identifiable parts of the finished product. Wastage (normal vs abnormal) is treated differently — normal wastage is absorbed into the material cost; abnormal wastage is charged to the Costing P&L Account.

**Step 2 — Direct Labour (DL) / Direct Wages:**
Wages paid to workers who are directly engaged in converting raw materials into finished goods. Includes basic pay, dearness allowance, incentive bonuses, and overtime attributable directly to production.

**Step 3 — Direct / Chargeable Expenses:**
Expenses specifically incurred for a particular job, contract, or process (e.g., hire of a special machine, patented design fees, royalties paid per unit).

**Step 4 — Prime Cost (PC):**
The aggregate of DM + DL + Direct Expenses. Represents the **"bare minimum"** cost that would not exist if the product were not made.

**Step 5 — Factory / Works Overheads:**
Indirect costs of operating the factory — factory rent, depreciation of plant, factory supervisor salaries, indirect materials, indirect labour, power, fuel, lighting. Added to Prime Cost to yield **Works Cost**.

**Step 6 — Office & Administrative Overheads:**
Costs of running the head office — managerial salaries, office rent, stationery, legal fees, audit fees. Added to Works Cost to yield **Cost of Production**.

## 2.2 Behavioural Classification — Fixed, Variable, Semi-Variable

**Fixed Costs:** Costs that remain constant in *total* (not per unit) over a relevant range of activity, irrespective of output. Example: factory rent of **₹50,000/month** stays the same whether the factory produces 100 or 1,000 units. *Per unit*, however, fixed cost **decreases** as output rises.

**Variable Costs:** Costs that vary in *direct proportion* to the volume of output. Example: raw material cost of **₹200 per unit** — total variable cost = 200 × N units.

**Semi-Variable Costs:** Costs that contain both a fixed and a variable component. Example: electricity bill = **₹10,000 fixed + ₹50 per unit consumed**. Requires the **High-Low Method** or **Least Squares Method** to split.

> [!NOTE]
> **The High-Low Method formula (KTU favourite):**
> - Variable Cost per unit = (Highest Activity Cost − Lowest Activity Cost) ÷ (Highest Activity Units − Lowest Activity Units)
> - Fixed Cost = Total Cost − (Variable Cost per unit × Activity Units)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PC = DM + DL + DE$ | Prime Cost | ₹ |
| 2 | $WC = PC + FOH$ | Works Cost | ₹ |
| 3 | $COP = WC + AOH$ | Cost of Production | ₹ |
| 4 | $TC = COP + S\&DOH$ | Total Cost | ₹ |
| 5 | $SP = TC + Profit$ | Selling Price | ₹ |
| 6 | $FC = TC - (VC \times Q)$ | Fixed Cost | ₹ |
| 7 | $VC_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{high} - C_{low}}{Q_{high} - Q_{low}}$ | Variable Cost/unit | ₹/unit |
| 8 | $BEP_{units} = \dfrac{FC}{SP_{unit} - VC_{unit}}$ | Break-Even Units | units |
| 9 | $BEP_{value} = \dfrac{FC}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$ | Break-Even Sales | ₹ |
| 10 | $P/V = \dfrac{Contribution}{Sales} \times 100$ | Profit/Volume Ratio | % |
| 11 | $Contribution = SP_{unit} - VC_{unit}$ | Per unit contribution | ₹/unit |
| 12 | $MOS = \dfrac{Profit}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$ | Margin of Safety | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend (used in all KTU problems):**
> - DM = Direct Material, DL = Direct Labour, DE = Direct Expenses
> - FOH = Factory Overheads, AOH = Administrative Overheads
> - S&DOH = Selling & Distribution Overheads
> - WC = Works Cost, PC = Prime Cost, COP = Cost of Production, TC = Total Cost
> - FC = Fixed Cost, VC = Variable Cost, Q = Quantity, SP = Selling Price
> - BEP = Break-Even Point, MOS = Margin of Safety

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In civil/mechanical project engineering, the **Cost of Production** principle is directly applied in:

- **Tendering & Estimation:** A contractor computes the unit cost of producing one cubic metre of concrete (DM + DL + machine hours + plant depreciation + site overheads) to quote a competitive yet profitable rate.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** In-house COP is compared with the supplier's quoted price.
- **Plant Utilisation Studies:** As output rises, fixed cost per unit falls — guiding the *economic batch quantity*.
- **Standard Costing & Variance Analysis:** Actual COP vs Standard COP → Material Variance, Labour Variance, Overhead Variance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost of Production Hierarchy

We start from the bottom-most element and build upward, just as KTU problems expect.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Prime Cost (PC)} \quad & PC = DM + DL + DE \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Add Factory Overheads} \quad & WC = PC + FOH \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Add Administrative Overheads} \quad & COP = WC + AOH \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Add Selling & Distribution OH} \quad & TC = COP + S\&DOH \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Add Profit Margin} \quad & SP = TC + \text{Profit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explained (Row by Row):**
- **Row 1:** Direct Material is the cost of all inputs that physically become part of the product. Direct Labour is the human effort directly applied. Direct Expenses are the special costs uniquely tied to this product. Their sum is the *Prime Cost* — the unavoidable floor cost.
- **Row 2:** The factory must operate (rent, lighting, supervisor salaries) regardless of output. These are *Factory Overheads*. Adding them converts Prime Cost into Works Cost — what it *costs the factory* to produce.
- **Row 3:** The head office (accounts, HR, MD's salary) costs money irrespective of the factory's activity level. Adding Administrative Overheads gives the *Cost of Production* — the complete cost incurred by the enterprise to produce the goods, sitting in the warehouse ready to be sold.
- **Row 4:** Once produced, goods must be packed, advertised, and delivered. These Selling & Distribution Overheads are added to obtain the *Total Cost / Cost of Sales*.
- **Row 5:** A margin is added to obtain the Selling Price quoted to the customer.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Typical — 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit provides the following data for the year ended 31-Mar-2024:

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Raw Materials Consumed | 4,00,000 |
| Direct Wages | 2,50,000 |
| Direct Expenses | 50,000 |
| Factory Rent & Taxes | 60,000 |
| Depreciation on Plant | 40,000 |
| Office Salaries | 55,000 |
| Office Rent | 20,000 |
| Selling Commission | 30,000 |
| Carriage Outward | 25,000 |
| Sales (10,000 units) | 12,00,000 |
| Profit | ? |

**Required:** (a) Prepare a Cost Sheet and find the Profit. (b) Calculate the Break-Even Point in units and the Margin of Safety.

### Part (a) — Cost Sheet Preparation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Material (Raw Materials Consumed)} \quad &= \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Direct Wages} \quad &= \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Direct Expenses} \quad &= \text{₹ } 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\hline
\textbf{PRIME COST} \quad &= \text{₹ } 7{,}00{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Add: Factory Rent \& Taxes} \quad &= \text{₹ } 60{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Add: Depreciation on Plant} \quad &= \text{₹ } 40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\hline
\textbf{WORKS COST} \quad &= \text{₹ } 8{,}00{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Add: Office Salaries} \quad &= \text{₹ } 55{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Add: Office Rent} \quad &= \text{₹ } 20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\hline
\textbf{COST OF PRODUCTION} \quad &= \text{₹ } 8{,}75{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Add: Selling Commission} \quad &= \text{₹ } 30{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Add: Carriage Outward} \quad &= \text{₹ } 25{,}000 \\[4pt]
\hline
\textbf{TOTAL COST} \quad &= \text{₹ } 9{,}30{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Add: Profit (Balancing Figure)} \quad &= \text{₹ } 2{,}70{,}000 \\[4pt]
\hline
\textbf{SALES} \quad &= \text{₹ } 12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Break-Even Point & Margin of Safety

**Step 1:** Separate Fixed and Variable costs. From the cost sheet:

- **Total Cost = ₹ 9,30,000** for 10,000 units
- **Variable Cost (assumed):** Direct Material + Direct Wages + Direct Expenses + Selling Commission (varies with sales) + Carriage Outward

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Variable Cost Total} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 25{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ } 7{,}55{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Variable Cost per unit} &= \dfrac{7{,}55{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{₹ } 75.50 \\[6pt]
\text{Fixed Cost Total} &= \text{Total Cost} - \text{Variable Cost Total} \\[4pt]
&= 9{,}30{,}000 - 7{,}55{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}75{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** Carriage outward and selling commission are treated as variable *only* when they are directly proportional to units sold (a simplifying assumption KTU explicitly tests). If they are fixed-period expenses, they are treated as fixed. The examiner accepts both, provided the student *declares the assumption*.

**Step 2:** Selling price per unit and Contribution per unit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Selling Price per unit} &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{₹ } 120 \\[6pt]
\text{Contribution per unit} &= SP - VC = 120 - 75.50 = \text{₹ } 44.50 \\[6pt]
\text{P/V Ratio} &= \dfrac{44.50}{120} \times 100 = 37.083\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Break-Even Point in units:

$$
BEP_{units} = \dfrac{FC}{Contribution \, per \, unit} = \dfrac{1{,}75{,}000}{44.50} = 3{,}932.58 \approx 3{,}933 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4:** Margin of Safety:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOS in units} &= \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales in units} \\[4pt]
&= 10{,}000 - 3{,}933 = 6{,}067 \text{ units} \\[6pt]
\text{MOS in value} &= 6{,}067 \times 120 = \text{₹ } 7{,}28{,}040
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative MOS formula using P/V ratio:** $MOS = \dfrac{Profit}{P/V \text{ ratio}} = \dfrac{2{,}70{,}000}{0.37083} = \text{₹ } 7{,}28{,}040$ — matches. This cross-verification technique is highly rewarded in KTU valuation.

## 3.3 Behavioural Decomposition — High-Low Method Derivation

Given two data points: (Q₁, C₁) and (Q₂, C₂), where Q₂ > Q₁:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Difference in Cost} \quad & \Delta C = C_2 - C_1 \\[4pt]
\text{Difference in Activity} \quad & \Delta Q = Q_2 - Q_1 \\[4pt]
\text{Variable Cost per unit} \quad & VC_{unit} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta Q} \\[4pt]
\text{Fixed Cost} \quad & FC = C_1 - (VC_{unit} \times Q_1) \\[4pt]
\text{Verification} \quad & FC = C_2 - (VC_{unit} \times Q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The variable portion of any semi-variable cost moves *in lockstep* with activity. The total movement in cost between two activity levels must therefore be entirely attributable to the variable component. The fixed component remains unchanged and is "back-calculated" by subtracting variable cost from either data point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost of Production — Hierarchical Buildup (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the flow of cost absorption from the lowest element to the final Selling Price. Each block is an "absorption node" — a layer of cost added to the running total.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[DM: Direct Material]
    node2[DL: Direct Labour]
    node3[DE: Direct Expenses]
    node4[PRIME COST Block]
    node5[FOH: Factory Overheads]
    node6[WORKS COST Block]
    node7[AOH: Administrative Overheads]
    node8[COST OF PRODUCTION Block]
    node9[SDOH: Selling and Distribution OH]
    node10[TOTAL COST Block]
    node11[PROFIT Margin]
    node12[SELLING PRICE Block]

    node1 --> node4
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12

    style node4 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    style node6 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    style node8 fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C00000,color:#000
    style node10 fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6,color:#000
    style node12 fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#548235,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow blocks (Prime Cost, Works Cost) are intermediate cost pools. The red-bordered block (Cost of Production) is the **KTU Module 3 focal point**. The blue and green blocks lead to the final customer-facing Selling Price.

## 4.2 Cost Behaviour Classification Flow

This diagram classifies the *element of cost* by its behaviour with respect to output volume — critical for managerial decision-making in projects.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Element of Cost] --> nodeB{Fixed or Variable}
    nodeB --> nodeC[FIXED COST: Constant in Total]
    nodeB --> nodeD[VARIABLE COST: Proportional to Output]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Factory Rent]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Depreciation]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Managerial Salary]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Insurance Premium]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Raw Material]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Direct Labour Piece Rate]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Sales Commission Pct]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Power Consumed Per Unit]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Semi-Variable Cost]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Telephone Charges]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Electricity Bill]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Maintenance Expenses]

    style nodeA fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6
    style nodeB fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656
    style nodeC fill:#F4B084,stroke:#C55A11
    style nodeD fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#548235
    style nodeE fill:#BDD7EE,stroke:#2E75B6
```

## 4.3 Break-Even Analysis — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    in1[Selling Price per unit] --> in2[Variable Cost per unit]
    in2 --> in3[Contribution per unit: SP minus VC]
    in3 --> in4[Fixed Cost Input]
    in4 --> in5[BEP in units: FC divided by Contribution]
    in5 --> out1[BEP in Rupees]
    in5 --> out2[Margin of Safety units]
    in5 --> out3[Profit at given output]

    style in3 fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#548235
    style in5 fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C00000
    style out1 fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6
    style out2 fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6
    style out3 fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mapping Note:** The red-bordered "BEP computation" node is the most frequently tested 14-mark question in the Cost of Production topic. The P/V ratio and Margin of Safety are derived outputs required for managerial decision-making.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between Prime Cost and Works Cost. Why is Administrative Overhead excluded from Prime Cost?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Prime Cost (1 Mark):** Aggregate of Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct/Chargeable Expenses. It represents the minimum cost that would be avoided if the product were not manufactured.
- **Works Cost (1 Mark):** Prime Cost + Factory/Works Overheads. It is the total cost of production incurred *within the factory* boundary, up to the point where goods are ready in the finished goods store.
- **Reason for exclusion (1 Mark):** Administrative Overheads are incurred for the *overall management of the business* (e.g., head office, HR, accounts), not for the conversion process inside the factory. They are incurred even if the factory is shut down for a month, hence they cannot be a part of Prime Cost which represents *manufacturing-only* essential cost.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the High-Low Method of cost separation. State one limitation."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Concept (1 Mark):** The High-Low Method is a simple two-point technique to split a semi-variable cost into its fixed and variable components, using the data of the highest and lowest activity periods.
- **Formula (1 Mark):** $VC_{unit} = (C_{high} - C_{low}) \div (Q_{high} - Q_{low})$, then $FC = C_{high} - (VC_{unit} \times Q_{high})$.
- **Limitation (1 Mark):** It considers only *two extreme data points*, ignoring all intermediate observations. If either extreme is non-representative (e.g., caused by a one-time strike or a freak order), the result is misleading. The Least Squares Method overcomes this.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer ONE of the two alternatives (Choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** From the following particulars, prepare a **Cost Sheet** showing Prime Cost, Works Cost, Cost of Production, Total Cost, and Profit for the year ending 31-Mar-2024. **(7 Marks)**

| Particulars | ₹ |
|---|---|
| Opening Raw Material | 60,000 |
| Purchases of Raw Material | 3,40,000 |
| Closing Raw Material | 50,000 |
| Direct Wages | 1,80,000 |
| Direct Expenses | 20,000 |
| Factory Power & Fuel | 35,000 |
| Factory Rent | 25,000 |
| Depreciation on Machinery | 30,000 |
| Office Expenses | 18,000 |
| Salaries to Office Staff | 42,000 |
| Selling Expenses | 28,000 |
| Carriage Outward | 12,000 |
| Sales (8,000 units) | 9,00,000 |

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Material Consumed** (1 Mark)
$$
\text{Material Consumed} = \text{Opening} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Closing} = 60{,}000 + 3{,}40{,}000 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Prime Cost** (2 Marks)
$$
PC = 3{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 5{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Works Cost** (1 Mark)
$$
WC = 5{,}50{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 30{,}000 = \text{₹ } 6{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Cost of Production** (1 Mark)
$$
COP = 6{,}40{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 42{,}000 = \text{₹ } 7{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Total Cost & Profit** (2 Marks)
$$
TC = 7{,}00{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 12{,}000 = \text{₹ } 7{,}40{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - TC = 9{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}40{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}60{,}000
$$

---

**(b)** From the above data, calculate the **Break-Even Point in units and in Rupees**. Also find the **Margin of Safety**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Variable Cost Separation** (2 Marks)
- Variable items: Material Consumed + Direct Wages + Direct Expenses + Selling Expenses + Carriage Outward
$$
VC_{total} = 3{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 12{,}000 = \text{₹ } 5{,}90{,}000
$$
$$
VC_{unit} = \dfrac{5{,}90{,}000}{8{,}000} = \text{₹ } 73.75
$$

**Step 2: Fixed Cost Calculation** (1 Mark)
$$
FC = TC - VC_{total} = 7{,}40{,}000 - 5{,}90{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3: SP/unit, Contribution/unit, P/V Ratio** (1 Mark)
$$
SP_{unit} = \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000}{8{,}000} = \text{₹ } 112.50
$$
$$
\text{Contribution/unit} = 112.50 - 73.75 = \text{₹ } 38.75
$$
$$
P/V = \dfrac{38.75}{112.50} \times 100 = 34.444\%
$$

**Step 4: BEP in units & Rupees** (2 Marks)
$$
BEP_{units} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{38.75} = 3{,}870.97 \approx 3{,}871 \text{ units}
$$
$$
BEP_{value} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{0.34444} = \text{₹ } 4{,}35{,}484
$$

**Step 5: Margin of Safety** (1 Mark)
$$
MOS_{units} = 8{,}000 - 3{,}871 = 4{,}129 \text{ units}
$$
$$
MOS_{value} = 4{,}129 \times 112.50 = \text{₹ } 4{,}64{,}513
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** A factory uses the High-Low Method and gives you the following data for the last six months:

| Month | Production Units | Maintenance Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 2,000 | 8,000 |
| Feb | 3,500 | 9,500 |
| Mar | 4,000 | 10,000 |
| Apr | 1,500 | 7,500 |
| May | 5,000 | 11,500 |
| Jun | 3,000 | 9,000 |

**Find:** (i) Variable Maintenance Cost per unit. (ii) Fixed Maintenance Cost per month. (iii) Estimated Maintenance Cost at a production level of 6,000 units. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify Highest and Lowest Activity** (1 Mark)
- Highest: May → Q = 5,000, C = ₹ 11,500
- Lowest: April → Q = 1,500, C = ₹ 7,500

**Step 2: Variable Cost per unit** (2 Marks)
$$
VC_{unit} = \dfrac{11{,}500 - 7{,}500}{5{,}000 - 1{,}500} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{3{,}500} = \text{₹ } 1.1429 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Step 3: Fixed Cost** (2 Marks)
$$
FC = 11{,}500 - (1.1429 \times 5{,}000) = 11{,}500 - 5{,}714.29 = \text{₹ } 5{,}785.71
$$
**Cross-check:** $FC = 7{,}500 - (1.1429 \times 1{,}500) = 7{,}500 - 1{,}714.29 = \text{₹ } 5{,}785.71$ ✓

**Step 4: Estimated Cost at 6,000 units** (2 Marks)
$$
TC = FC + (VC_{unit} \times Q) = 5{,}785.71 + (1.1429 \times 6{,}000) = 5{,}785.71 + 6{,}857.14 = \text{₹ } 12{,}642.86
$$

---

**(b)** Define **Cost of Production**. With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **classification of costs by behaviour** with engineering project examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

- **Definition of Cost of Production (2 Marks):** *The aggregate expenditure incurred by an enterprise on Direct Material, Direct Labour, Direct Expenses, Factory Overheads, and Administrative Overheads to bring a product or project deliverable to a finished, saleable state.* It is the cost of goods manufactured and transferred to the finished goods store.
- **Classification by Behaviour (4 Marks):**
  - **Fixed Costs (1 Mark):** Constant in total irrespective of output. *Project Example:* Site office rent for a 24-month construction project — ₹80,000/month — same whether 1,000 or 5,000 cubic metres of concrete are poured.
  - **Variable Costs (1 Mark):** Vary directly with output volume. *Project Example:* Cement cost of ₹450 per cubic metre of concrete — total cost = ₹450 × volume poured.
  - **Semi-Variable Costs (1 Mark):** Contain both fixed and variable elements. *Project Example:* Site electricity bill = ₹15,000 fixed monthly charge + ₹8 per unit consumed by welding machines and batching plant.
- **Why classification matters (1 Mark):** It enables the project manager to compute the Break-Even Point, evaluate Make-or-Buy alternatives, and perform Variance Analysis during project execution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Cost of Production" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Carriage Outward with Carriage Inward:** Carriage Inward is a *Direct Expense* (added to Prime Cost). Carriage Outward is a *Selling & Distribution Overhead* (added *after* Cost of Production). Mixing these up costs **2 to 3 marks** outright.
> 2. **Forgetting Opening/Closing Stock adjustments in Material:** Raw Material Consumed = Opening + Purchases − Closing. Students often ignore the closing stock and lose a full mark.
> 3. **Wrong sign for Abnormal Wastage:** Normal wastage is *absorbed* into material cost; abnormal wastage is *charged to the P&L account* (NOT to COP). This is a classic KTU trick question.
> 4. **P/V ratio as a percentage vs decimal:** KTU expects you to use the *decimal* form (e.g., 0.37) in the BEP-in-rupees formula. Using the percentage form (37) gives a 100× error.
> 5. **Missing units in the final answer:** BEP must be quoted in *units* and *Rupees separately*. Margin of Safety must be quoted in both *units* and *value*. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit/dimension.
> 6. **Treating selling commission as fixed:** It must be treated as a *variable* percentage of sales unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cost of Production (Module 3):**
> - **Hierarchy to memorise (bottom-up):** DM + DL + DE = **Prime Cost** → add FOH = **Works Cost** → add AOH = **Cost of Production** → add S&DOH = **Total Cost** → add Profit = **Selling Price**.
> - **Carriage Inward → Direct Expense.** **Carriage Outward → Selling Overhead.** This is the single most-tested distinction.
> - **Three Behavioural Categories:** Fixed (constant in total), Variable (proportional to output), Semi-Variable (mixed). The **High-Low Method** is the standard 2-point splitter for semi-variable costs.
> - **Contribution Margin:** $SP - VC$. It is the engine of BEP and P/V ratio analysis.
> - **P/V Ratio:** $Contribution \div Sales \times 100$. A higher P/V ratio means profits grow faster with sales.
> - **BEP Units:** $FC \div Contribution_{unit}$. **BEP Value:** $FC \div P/V \text{ ratio (decimal)}$.
> - **Margin of Safety:** Actual Sales − BEP Sales. A wide MOS indicates low business risk.
> - **Profit = $MOS \times P/V \text{ ratio}$** — a one-line cross-check that KTU examiners love.
> - **Wastage rule:** Normal wastage → absorbed. Abnormal wastage → P&L charge.
> - **Apportionment rule:** When overheads benefit multiple products, apportion on a fair basis (floor area, labour hours, machine hours, direct wages).
> - **Cost Sheet is always vertical** in KTU answers, with each subtotal clearly boxed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Profitability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT631 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Profitability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Profitability in Project Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Profitability* in project engineering is the quantitative measure of a project's ability to generate financial returns (gains) in excess of its costs, investments, and operational expenses over its useful economic life. It is the **principal criterion** used to evaluate whether a proposed engineering project (capital investment) is financially viable and worth undertaking.

Unlike *profit* (which is an absolute monetary figure such as ₹50 Lakhs per year), *profitability* is a **relative measure** — typically expressed as a ratio, percentage, or time-based index. It answers the question:

> "For every Rupee invested, how much return is generated, and how quickly?"

## 1.2 Why is Profitability Analysis Critical for Engineers?

Every KTU 2024 Scheme engineering graduate involved in design, manufacturing, construction, or project management will, at some point, face a **Capital Budgeting Decision** — Should we invest ₹500 Crore in a new plant, or expand the existing one? Profitability analysis tools give us a mathematically rigorous answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** A project is *acceptable* only if its profitability metrics (ROI, NPV, IRR, BCR) satisfy a pre-defined *hurdle rate* set by the firm's finance team. For public-sector projects (KTU frequently tests these), the *social discount rate* is used (typically **8%–12%** in India).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏪

Imagine you run a small tea stall:
- You invest **₹10,000** in a vending cart, cups, sugar, and milk.
- Each day, you earn **₹500** in revenue and spend **₹300** in ingredients and gas.
- **Daily profit** = ₹500 − ₹300 = **₹200**.
- **Profitability** is *not* just the ₹200. Profitability asks:
  - "Out of my ₹10,000, what % am I earning back each day?" → **ROI = 2% per day**.
  - "After how many days will I recover my ₹10,000?" → **Payback Period = 50 days**.
  - "Is ₹10,000 today worth more than the future profits it will bring?" → **Time Value of Money → NPV**.

In engineering, the "tea stall" is a **hydroelectric dam, a flyover, a factory, or a software product**. The same logic scales up.

## 1.4 Standard Profitability Metrics in KTU Syllabus

| # | Metric | Type | One-Line Meaning |
|---|--------|------|------------------|
| 1 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | Static | % of profit per unit of investment |
| 2 | **Payback Period (PBP)** | Static | Time to recover original investment |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | Dynamic | Today's value of all future cash flows minus investment |
| 4 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | Dynamic | Discount rate that makes NPV = 0 |
| 5 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | Dynamic | Ratio of present worth of benefits to present worth of costs |
| 6 | **Profitability Index (PI)** | Dynamic | NPV per Rupee of investment |
| 7 | **Break-Even Analysis** | Static | Sales volume where total revenue = total cost |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Profitability Methods

Profitability analysis techniques are divided into two broad families based on whether they account for the **Time Value of Money (TVM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Value of Money (TVM):** A Rupee *today* is worth more than a Rupee *tomorrow* because today's Rupee can be invested to earn interest, and inflation erodes the future Rupee's purchasing power. **Any realistic engineering project must use TVM-based (dynamic) methods.**

### A. Static Methods (Ignore TVM)
These treat all cash flows as if a 2024 Rupee and a 2034 Rupee are equal. They are simple, fast, and used for *preliminary screening*.

1. **Return on Investment (ROI) / Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)**
2. **Payback Period (PBP)**

### B. Dynamic Methods (Incorporate TVM)
These discount future cash flows to their *present equivalent* using a discount rate `i` (the *cost of capital* or *Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return – MARR*).

1. **Net Present Value (NPV)**
2. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**
3. **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)**
4. **Profitability Index (PI)**

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanics of Each Method

### 2.2.1 Return on Investment (ROI)

**Concept:** Measures the average annual accounting profit as a percentage of the total investment.

- **Why use it?** Quick, intuitive, comparable across projects of different sizes.
- **How it works:** Average annual net income is divided by the average book value of the investment.
- **Decision rule:** Accept the project if `ROI ≥ MARR (Hurdle Rate)`.

### 2.2.2 Payback Period (PBP)

**Concept:** The exact time (in years) required for cumulative cash inflows to equal the initial investment.

- **Why use it?** It explicitly measures **liquidity risk** — how quickly capital is recovered.
- **How it works:** Add yearly cash flows until the sum equals the initial outlay. The year in which this occurs is the payback period.
- **Decision rule:** Accept if `PBP ≤ Target Payback Period` (often 3–5 years for industrial projects).

### 2.2.3 Net Present Value (NPV)

**Concept:** Converts every future cash flow into its *equivalent value at time zero* (the present) using the discount rate `i`, then sums them up. Subtracts the initial investment to get a single number.

- **Why use it?** Gold standard. Directly measures **wealth creation** in today's Rupees.
- **How it works:** `NPV = Σ [CFₜ / (1+i)ᵗ] − I₀`
- **Decision rule:** Accept if `NPV ≥ 0`. If two mutually exclusive projects, choose the one with **higher NPV**.

### 2.2.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

**Concept:** The discount rate `i*` at which the NPV of a project is **exactly zero**. It is the *project's own earning rate*.

- **Why use it?** Expresses profitability as a single percentage — easy to compare with the firm's cost of capital.
- **How it works:** Solve `Σ [CFₜ / (1+i*)ᵗ] = I₀` for `i*`.
- **Decision rule:** Accept if `IRR ≥ MARR`.

### 2.2.5 Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

**Concept:** Ratio of the present worth of *all benefits* to the present worth of *all costs* (including the initial investment).

- **Why use it?** Standard for **public-sector and government projects** (roads, bridges, irrigation).
- **How it works:** `BCR = PW(Benefits) / PW(Costs)`
- **Decision rule:** Accept if `BCR ≥ 1.0`.

### 2.2.6 Profitability Index (PI)

**Concept:** A normalized form of NPV — present value of future cash flows **per Rupee** of initial investment.

- **Why use it?** Useful for **capital rationing** (when the firm has a fixed budget and must rank many small projects).
- **How it works:** `PI = PV of future cash flows / I₀`
- **Decision rule:** Accept if `PI ≥ 1.0`. Rank projects by PI descending.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme problems:**
> - `I₀` = Initial Investment (made at time t = 0)
> - `CFₜ` = Net Cash Flow in year `t` (Revenue − Operating Cost)
> - `Sₙ` = Salvage Value at the end of project life `n`
> - `i` = Discount rate / MARR / Cost of Capital (in decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10%)
> - `n` = Project life in years
> - `P` = Present Value, `F` = Future Value, `A` = Annuity (uniform annual amount)

| Method | Formula | Decision Criterion |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| **Simple Payback Period** | $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{I_0}{\text{Annual CF}}$ (for uniform CF) | PBP ≤ Target |
| **ROI / ARR** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$ | ROI ≥ MARR |
| **NPV** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | NPV ≥ 0 |
| **NPV (uniform CF)** | $\text{NPV} = -I_0 + A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} + \dfrac{S_n}{(1+i)^n}$ | NPV ≥ 0 |
| **IRR** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$ | IRR ≥ MARR |
| **BCR** | $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$ | BCR ≥ 1 |
| **PI** | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}}{I_0}$ | PI ≥ 1 |
| **Present Worth Factor (P/A, i, n)** | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | — |
| **Future Worth Factor (F/A, i, n)** | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | — |
| **Single Payment Present Worth (P/F, i, n)** | $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ | — |
| **Capital Recovery (A/P, i, n)** | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | — |
| **Sinking Fund (A/F, i, n)** | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Civil Engineering:** BCR is mandatory for any highway or irrigation project funded by NHAI, World Bank, or ADB.
- **Mechanical/Industrial:** ROI and Payback are used to evaluate new CNC machines, robotics, or factory automation.
- **Electrical/Power:** IRR and NPV decide whether to install a 100 MW solar farm.
- **Software/Tech:** PI ranks multiple small R&D projects within a fixed innovation budget.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why NPV Formula Takes Its Form

The Time Value of Money states that money has an *earning potential*. If the discount rate is `i` (10% per year), then ₹100 received 1 year from now is equivalent to:

$$P_0 = \dfrac{F_1}{1+i} = \dfrac{100}{1.10} = 90.91$$

If the same ₹100 is received 2 years from now, we discount *twice*:

$$P_0 = \dfrac{F_2}{(1+i)^2} = \dfrac{100}{(1.10)^2} = 82.64$$

Generalizing for any year `t`:

$$P_0 = \dfrac{F_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

Now, if we have *multiple* cash flows `CF₁, CF₂, …, CFₙ` at the end of years 1 through `n`, the total present value of all future inflows is the sum:

$$\text{PV of inflows} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

The **Net Present Value** subtracts the initial investment `I₀` (which is already at time zero, so it does not need discounting):

$$\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

This is the foundational equation of modern capital budgeting. We can also include a salvage value `Sₙ` recovered at the end of year `n`:

$$\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} + \dfrac{S_n}{(1+i)^n}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Special Case — Uniform Annual Cash Flow

If the project generates a **constant annual cash flow `A`** for `n` years, we can factor out `A` from the sum:

$$\text{NPV} = -I_0 + A \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t}$$

The summation $\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t}$ is a standard geometric series with ratio $\dfrac{1}{1+i}$. Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{t=0}^{n-1} r^t = \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$, we get:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

This expression is universally tabulated as the **Present Worth Annuity Factor**, denoted `(P/A, i, n)`. Therefore, for uniform cash flows:

$$\text{NPV} = -I_0 + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + S_n \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-Style)

### Example 1: Payback Period (Uniform CF)

**Problem:** A small-scale manufacturing unit requires an initial investment of ₹8,00,000. It is expected to generate a uniform net cash flow of ₹2,00,000 per year. Calculate the payback period.

**Solution:**
- Each year, ₹2,00,000 is recovered.
- To recover ₹8,00,000, we need `8,00,000 ÷ 2,00,000 = 4` years.
- **PBP = 4 years.**
- If the target payback is 5 years, the project is **acceptable**.

### Example 2: NPV with Uniform Cash Flow + Salvage

**Problem:** Initial investment = ₹10,00,000. Annual net cash flow = ₹3,00,000 for 5 years. Salvage value at end of year 5 = ₹1,00,000. MARR = 10%. Find NPV.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the factors (using standard tables or calculation):
- `(P/A, 10%, 5) = [(1.10)⁵ − 1] / [0.10 × (1.10)⁵]`
- `(1.10)⁵ = 1.61051`
- `(P/A, 10%, 5) = (1.61051 − 1) / (0.10 × 1.61051) = 0.61051 / 0.161051 = 3.7908`
- `(P/F, 10%, 5) = 1 / 1.61051 = 0.6209`

Step 2 — Apply the NPV formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -I_0 + A \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 5) + S_5 \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 5) \\
&= -10{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6209 \\
&= -10{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}37{,}240 + 62{,}090 \\
&= +1{,}99{,}330
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** NPV = +₹1,99,330 > 0, so the project is **financially viable**.

### Example 3: IRR by Interpolation

**Problem:** Initial investment = ₹5,00,000. Cash inflows: Year 1 = ₹1,50,000, Year 2 = ₹2,00,000, Year 3 = ₹2,50,000, Year 4 = ₹2,00,000. MARR = 12%. Find IRR.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Set up the NPV = 0 equation:

$$0 = -5{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1+i)} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1+i)^2} + \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{(1+i)^3} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1+i)^4}$$

Step 2 — Trial 1: Try `i = 18%`:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{18\%} &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.18} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.3924} + \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.6430} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.9388} \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}27{,}119 + 1{,}43{,}637 + 1{,}52{,}162 + 1{,}03{,}156 \\
&= +26{,}074
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Trial 2: Try `i = 20%`:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{20\%} &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.20} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.44} + \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.728} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{2.0736} \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000 + 1{,}38{,}889 + 1{,}44{,}676 + 96{,}450 \\
&= +5{,}015
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — Trial 3: Try `i = 21%`:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{21\%} &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.21} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} + \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.7716} + \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{2.1436} \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}23{,}967 + 1{,}36{,}603 + 1{,}41{,}117 + 93{,}300 \\
&= -5{,}013
\end{aligned}$$

Step 5 — Linear Interpolation between 20% (NPV = +5,015) and 21% (NPV = −5,013):

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \dfrac{5{,}015}{5{,}015 + 5{,}013} \times (21\% - 20\%) = 20\% + 0.500 \times 1\% = 20.50\%$$

**Interpretation:** IRR = 20.50% > MARR (12%), so the project is **strongly acceptable**.

### Example 4: Benefit-Cost Ratio (Public Project)

**Problem:** A new rural road project has the following discounted values (at 8% social discount rate):
- PW of Benefits = ₹25 Crore
- PW of Costs (including initial) = ₹20 Crore

**Solution:**

$$\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\text{PW of Benefits}}{\text{PW of Costs}} = \dfrac{25}{20} = 1.25$$

**Interpretation:** BCR = 1.25 > 1.0, so the project is **socially and economically viable**.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Profitability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Project Proposal] --> B[Estimate Initial Investment I0]
    B --> C[Forecast Annual Cash Flows CFt for n years]
    C --> D[Determine MARR / Discount Rate i]
    D --> E{Select Profitability Method}
    E --> F[Static Methods ROI and PBP]
    E --> G[Dynamic Methods NPV IRR BCR PI]
    F --> H[Compute ROI]
    F --> I[Compute PBP]
    G --> J[Compute NPV at MARR]
    G --> K[Compute IRR by iteration]
    G --> L[Compute BCR]
    G --> M[Compute PI]
    H --> N[Decision Check ROI vs MARR]
    I --> O[Decision Check PBP vs Target]
    J --> P[Decision Check NPV greater than 0]
    K --> Q[Decision Check IRR vs MARR]
    L --> R[Decision Check BCR vs 1.0]
    M --> S[Decision Check PI vs 1.0]
    N --> T{Accept or Reject}
    O --> T
    P --> T
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U[Project Recommended for Execution]
    T --> V[Project Rejected or Redesigned]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for NPV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Project Cash Flow Stream] --> B[Choose Discount Rate i]
    B --> C[Discount Each CFt to Present]
    C --> D[Sum All Present Values]
    D --> E[Subtract Initial Investment I0]
    E --> F[Compute NPV]
    F --> G{NPV Value}
    G --> H[NPV greater than 0 Accept]
    G --> I[NPV equals 0 Indifferent]
    G --> J[NPV less than 0 Reject]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Comparison of Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Static["STATIC METHODS No TVM"]
        A1[ROI]
        A2[Payback Period]
    end
    subgraph Dynamic["DYNAMIC METHODS With TVM"]
        B1[NPV]
        B2[IRR]
        B3[BCR]
        B4[Profitability Index]
    end
    Static --> C[Preliminary Screening]
    Dynamic --> D[Final Investment Decision]
    C --> E[Final KTU Board Report]
    D --> E
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between Profit and Profitability. Why is profitability analysis preferred over absolute profit figures for evaluating engineering projects?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Profit** is an *absolute monetary amount* (e.g., ₹50 Lakhs per year) representing the surplus of revenue over cost.
- **Profitability** is a *relative measure* (ratio, percentage, or time-based index) that expresses the efficiency of investment — i.e., how much profit is generated *per Rupee invested* or *per unit time*.
- Profitability is preferred because:
  1. It allows **comparison of projects of different sizes** (a ₹1 Cr project earning ₹10 L is more profitable than a ₹100 Cr project earning ₹50 Cr).
  2. It is **scale-independent** and meaningful to investors.
  3. It accounts for **risk, time, and opportunity cost** (in dynamic methods).
  4. It directly aligns with the firm's **wealth maximization objective**.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the Payback Period method? State its two major limitations.**

**Model Answer:**
- The **Payback Period (PBP)** is the time required for the cumulative cash inflows from a project to recover the original investment.
- **Limitations:**
  1. **Ignores the Time Value of Money** — treats a 2024 Rupee equal to a 2034 Rupee.
  2. **Ignores cash flows after the payback period** — a project that pays back in 3 years but generates nothing for the next 7 years is rated equal to one paying back in 3 years with strong long-term returns.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A manufacturing company is evaluating a proposal to install a new automated assembly line. The initial investment is **₹15,00,000**, and the project is expected to generate a **uniform net annual cash flow of ₹4,50,000** for **6 years**. At the end of year 6, the equipment will have a **salvage value of ₹1,50,000**. The company's Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR) is **12% per annum**.
> 
> **(a)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of the project. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** Compute the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of the project using the interpolation method, and state whether the project should be accepted. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — NPV Calculation (7 marks)

Step 1 — Identify the required factors at i = 12%:
- `(1.12)⁶ = 1.97382`
- `(P/A, 12%, 6) = (1.97382 − 1) / (0.12 × 1.97382) = 0.97382 / 0.23686 = 4.1114`
- `(P/F, 12%, 6) = 1 / 1.97382 = 0.5066`

Step 2 — Apply the NPV formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -I_0 + A \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 6) + S_6 \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 6) \\
&= -15{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \times 4.1114 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5066 \\
&= -15{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}50{,}130 + 75{,}990 \\
&= +4{,}26{,}120
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — **Conclusion:** NPV = **+₹4,26,120**, which is positive. The project is **acceptable** at MARR = 12%.

#### Part (b) — IRR Calculation (7 marks)

Step 1 — Set NPV = 0:

$$0 = -15{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \cdot (P/A, i, 6) + 1{,}50{,}000 \cdot (P/F, i, 6)$$

Step 2 — Trial 1: Try `i = 25%`:
- `(P/A, 25%, 6) = (1.25⁶ − 1) / (0.25 × 1.25⁶) = (3.8147 − 1) / (0.25 × 3.8147) = 2.8147 / 0.9537 = 2.9514`
- `(P/F, 25%, 6) = 1 / 3.8147 = 0.2621`
- `NPV = −15,00,000 + 4,50,000 × 2.9514 + 1,50,000 × 0.2621 = −15,00,000 + 13,28,130 + 39,315 = −1,32,555`

Step 3 — Trial 2: Try `i = 20%`:
- `(P/A, 20%, 6) = (2.0736 − 1) / (0.20 × 2.0736) = 1.0736 / 0.4147 = 2.5890`
- `(P/F, 20%, 6) = 1 / 2.0736 = 0.4823`
- `NPV = −15,00,000 + 4,50,000 × 2.5890 + 1,50,000 × 0.4823 = −15,00,000 + 11,65,050 + 72,345 = +2,62,395`

Step 4 — Interpolate between 20% and 25%:

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \dfrac{2{,}62{,}395}{2{,}62{,}395 + 1{,}32{,}555} \times (25\% - 20\%)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \dfrac{2{,}62{,}395}{3{,}94{,}950} \times 5\% = 20\% + 0.6647 \times 5\% = 20\% + 3.32\% = 23.32\%$$

Step 5 — **Conclusion:** IRR ≈ **23.32%**, which is significantly greater than the MARR of 12%. The project is **strongly acceptable** and should be implemented.

> ### Question B (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** A government agency is considering two mutually exclusive water-supply schemes for a rural district. Both schemes have a life of **10 years** and a social discount rate of **10%** applies.
> 
> | Parameter | Scheme X | Scheme Y |
> |-----------|----------|----------|
> | Initial Investment | ₹80,00,000 | ₹1,20,00,000 |
> | Annual Benefits | ₹15,00,000 | ₹22,00,000 |
> | Annual O&M Cost | ₹3,00,000 | ₹4,00,000 |
> | Salvage Value | ₹5,00,000 | ₹8,00,000 |
> 
> **(a)** Compute the **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** for both schemes and recommend which one should be selected. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** for both schemes and verify whether the BCR-based recommendation is consistent. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — BCR Calculation (7 marks)

Step 1 — Compute Net Annual Benefit (NAB) for each scheme:
- Scheme X: `NAB = 15,00,000 − 3,00,000 = ₹12,00,000`
- Scheme Y: `NAB = 22,00,000 − 4,00,000 = ₹18,00,000`

Step 2 — Find the factors at i = 10%, n = 10:
- `(P/A, 10%, 10) = [(1.10)¹⁰ − 1] / [0.10 × (1.10)¹⁰]`
- `(1.10)¹⁰ = 2.5937`
- `(P/A, 10%, 10) = (2.5937 − 1) / (0.10 × 2.5937) = 1.5937 / 0.25937 = 6.1446`
- `(P/F, 10%, 10) = 1 / 2.5937 = 0.3855`

Step 3 — Compute Present Worth of Benefits (Annual NAB × P/A + Salvage × P/F):
- `PW_Benefits (X) = 12,00,000 × 6.1446 + 5,00,000 × 0.3855 = 73,73,520 + 1,92,750 = 75,66,270`
- `PW_Benefits (Y) = 18,00,000 × 6.1446 + 8,00,000 × 0.3855 = 1,10,60,280 + 3,08,400 = 1,13,68,680`

Step 4 — Total PW of Costs = Initial Investment (since O&M is already netted out in the benefit stream, we add the initial investment back):

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used:** Since Annual Benefits and O&M are given separately, the BCR formula becomes:
> `BCR = (PW of Gross Benefits) / (Initial Investment + PW of O&M Costs)`
> This is the *gross-BCR* convention preferred by KTU examiners.

- `PW of Gross Benefits (X) = 15,00,000 × 6.1446 = 92,16,900`
- `PW of O&M Costs (X) = 3,00,000 × 6.1446 = 18,43,380`
- `Total PW of Costs (X) = 80,00,000 + 18,43,380 = 98,43,380`
- `BCR(X) = 92,16,900 / 98,43,380 = 0.936`

- `PW of Gross Benefits (Y) = 22,00,000 × 6.1446 = 1,35,18,120`
- `PW of O&M Costs (Y) = 4,00,000 × 6.1446 = 24,57,840`
- `Total PW of Costs (Y) = 1,20,00,000 + 24,57,840 = 1,44,57,840`
- `BCR(Y) = 1,35,18,120 / 1,44,57,840 = 0.935`

Step 5 — **Conclusion:** Both schemes have BCR < 1.0, indicating they are **not viable** under the strict BCR test. However, if forced to choose between two non-viable options, Scheme X is marginally better (0.936 > 0.935). The agency should **re-evaluate costs and benefits or seek a higher subsidy**.

#### Part (b) — NPV Verification (7 marks)

Step 1 — NPV using the NAB approach:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_X &= -80{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1446 + 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3855 \\
&= -80{,}00{,}000 + 73{,}73{,}520 + 1{,}92{,}750 \\
&= -4{,}33{,}730
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_Y &= -1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1446 + 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3855 \\
&= -1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}10{,}60{,}280 + 3{,}08{,}400 \\
&= -6{,}31{,}320
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2 — **Conclusion:** NPV_X (−₹4,33,730) is **less negative** than NPV_Y (−₹6,31,320). Scheme X is preferred (it destroys the *least* value). This is consistent with the BCR result that Scheme X is marginally better. However, since both NPVs are negative, the firm should **reject both** in a profit-maximizing context.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing gross and net BCR conventions** — KTU expects you to state which convention you are using. If O&M is netted out from benefits, the *initial investment must be added back to the denominator*.
> 2. **IRR interpolation sign error** — Always ensure NPV changes sign between your two trial rates. If both NPVs are positive, you must increase `i`; if both are negative, you must decrease `i`.
> 3. **Forgetting the salvage value** — Many students compute NPV for only `n` years of cash flow and ignore the end-of-life salvage. This typically costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Using the wrong time reference** — All present-worth calculations must be referenced to t = 0. Salvage is discounted for `n` years, not `n−1`.
> 5. **Confusing MARR with inflation** — The MARR is a *real* (or sometimes nominal) discount rate. Mixing it with cash flows that include inflation will give a wrong answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **Profitability ≠ Profit.** Profit is absolute; profitability is relative and decision-oriented.
- **Static methods** (ROI, PBP) are *fast but flawed* because they ignore the time value of money. They are best for **screening only**.
- **Dynamic methods** (NPV, IRR, BCR, PI) are *rigorous and TVM-compliant* — these are what KTU 2024 Scheme board questions primarily test.
- **NPV is the gold standard** for project acceptance. NPV ≥ 0 → Accept.
- **IRR** is the project's intrinsic rate of return. IRR ≥ MARR → Accept.
- **For mutually exclusive projects**, use NPV (not IRR) to choose — IRR can mislead in such cases (reinvestment rate assumption).
- **BCR ≥ 1.0** is the criterion for public-sector / government projects in India.
- **PI ≥ 1.0** is used when the firm has a **capital rationing** constraint.
- **Salvage value, working capital recovery, and tax effects** are common "trap" elements in KTU numerical problems — never ignore them.
- **Always state your decision criterion explicitly** in the exam (e.g., "Since NPV = +₹4,26,120 > 0, the project is recommended").
- **The Geometric Series Derivation** (Section 3.2) is the most important mathematical foundation — practice deriving `(P/A, i, n)` from scratch.
- **Master the table of factors** `(P/A, i, n)`, `(P/F, i, n)`, `(F/P, i, n)`, `(A/P, i, n)` — KTU 2024 allows non-programmable scientific calculators, and these factors are typically given in the question paper appendix, but you must know how to derive them if they are not.
- **Typical Indian MARR for industrial projects:** 12%–15%. **Social discount rate for government projects:** 8%–10%.
- **Always show 4–5 step derivations** with clean labeling. KTU examiners award *method marks* even when arithmetic is imperfect.
- **NPV and IRR can give conflicting signals** for *mutually exclusive projects* with different scales or timings. KTU prefers **NPV** as the tie-breaker.
- **A positive NPV means the project earns MORE than the MARR**, i.e., it creates *shareholder wealth*.
- **Cash flow ≠ Profit.** Profit includes non-cash items (depreciation). Use **cash flow** in all profitability calculations.
- **Sensitivity Analysis** (varying `i` or `CFₜ` by ±20%) is often a follow-up part-(b) question in KTU papers — practice computing NPV at different discount rates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Break-Even Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT631 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Break-Even Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Break-Even Analysis (BEA)** is a fundamental financial and managerial accounting technique used in **Project Engineering** and **Cost Management** to determine the point at which **Total Revenue (TR)** exactly equals **Total Cost (TC)**. At this specific juncture, the project yields **Zero Profit and Zero Loss**, mathematically expressed as **TR = TC** or **Profit (P) = 0**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Break-Even Analysis is treated as a **decision-making tool** that helps engineers and managers evaluate the financial viability, risk exposure, and operational cushion of an industrial or engineering project before capital commitment.

The three critical financial variables involved are:
- **Fixed Costs (FC)** — Costs that remain **constant regardless of production volume** (e.g., rent, salaries, depreciation, insurance).
- **Variable Costs (VC)** — Costs that **vary directly and proportionately** with production volume (e.g., raw materials, direct labor, packaging).
- **Sales Revenue (S)** — Income generated from selling the output at a given price.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tea Stall Start-Up"**
>
> Imagine a college student, *Arjun*, who borrows ₹10,000 from his father to open a small tea stall near the campus gate. This ₹10,000 covers his stall rent (one-time), a gas stove, cups, and a kettle. This is his **Fixed Cost** — whether he sells 1 cup or 1000 cups, he has already committed this amount.
>
> For every cup of tea he sells, he spends ₹5 on milk, sugar, tea powder, and gas. This is his **Variable Cost** per cup. He sells each cup for ₹15. So his **Contribution per cup** is ₹15 − ₹5 = **₹10**.
>
> Now, the question is: **"How many cups must Arjun sell just to get his father's money back?"**
>
> That number of cups is his **Break-Even Point**.
>
> $$\text{BEP (units)} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Contribution per unit}} = \frac{10{,}000}{10} = 1{,}000 \text{ cups}$$
>
> Until Arjun sells 1,000 cups, he is operating at a **loss**. The 1,001st cup onwards is pure **profit zone**. This is the essence of Break-Even Analysis.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **standard KTU engineering economics metrics** must be memorized verbatim for the End Semester Examination:
>
> - **Break-Even Point (BEP)** — The volume of sales where profit = **0**.
> - **Contribution Margin** — Sales minus Variable Cost.
> - **P/V Ratio (Profit Volume Ratio)** — Contribution expressed as a percentage of Sales.
> - **Margin of Safety (MoS)** — The buffer between actual sales and break-even sales.
> - **Angle of Incidence** — The angle at which the profit line cuts the break-even chart, indicating margin strength.
> - **Cash Break-Even Point** — Break-even point calculated using only **cash fixed costs** (excluding non-cash expenses like depreciation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic of Break-Even Analysis

Break-Even Analysis rests on a clear **cost behavior classification** of all expenses incurred in an engineering project. The logic unfolds in the following structured steps:

1. **Classify Costs:** All project costs are bifurcated into two mutually exclusive categories — **Fixed Costs (FC)** and **Variable Costs (VC)**.
2. **Establish Revenue Function:** Sales Revenue `S` is modeled as a **linear function** of quantity sold `Q` at a constant unit selling price `p`.
3. **Build Cost Function:** Total Cost `TC` is the **algebraic sum** of FC and a linear variable cost function `v × Q`.
4. **Set Profit Equation to Zero:** Equate Sales to Total Cost and solve for `Q` — the resulting value is the **Break-Even Quantity (BEP_Q)**.
5. **Compute Break-Even Sales (BEP_S):** Multiply BEP_Q by unit selling price to obtain the break-even sales in monetary terms.
6. **Measure Risk Cushion:** Compute **Margin of Safety (MoS)** to understand how much sales can drop before losses begin.
7. **Graphical Visualization:** Plot the **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) graph** to visually identify the break-even intersection.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — KTU Cognitive Depth

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is BEA important in Project Engineering?**
>
> Because every engineering project — be it a manufacturing plant, an IT system rollout, or a construction contract — has a **financial threshold** below which it is unviable. BEA quantifies that threshold with mathematical precision, enabling managers to:
>
> - **Set realistic sales targets** for the project team.
> - **Negotiate loans** by showing bankers the minimum performance level.
> - **Evaluate risk** by understanding the margin of safety.
> - **Compare alternative projects** by comparing their break-even volumes.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **entire formula repertoire** for Break-Even Analysis as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus. All formulae are mathematically verifiable and represent the **highest-yield** content for the ESE.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Cost | $TC = FC + (VC \times Q)$ | Currency (₹) |
| 2 | Total Revenue (Sales) | $S = p \times Q$ | Currency (₹) |
| 3 | Contribution per unit | $C_u = p - v$ | Currency per unit (₹/unit) |
| 4 | Total Contribution | $C = S - VC$ | Currency (₹) |
| 5 | BEP in Units | $BEP_Q = \dfrac{FC}{p - v} = \dfrac{FC}{C_u}$ | Number of units |
| 6 | BEP in Rupees | $BEP_S = \dfrac{FC}{P/V \text{ Ratio}}$ | Currency (₹) |
| 7 | P/V Ratio (Profit-Volume) | $P/V = \dfrac{C}{S} \times 100 = \dfrac{p - v}{p} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 8 | Profit Equation | $P = S \times (P/V) - FC$ | Currency (₹) |
| 9 | Margin of Safety (absolute) | $MoS = \text{Actual Sales} - BEP_S$ | Currency (₹) |
| 10 | Margin of Safety (%) | $MoS\% = \dfrac{MoS}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 11 | Cash BEP | $BEP_{cash} = \dfrac{\text{Cash Fixed Costs}}{C_u}$ | Number of units |
| 12 | Angle of Incidence | $\tan \theta = \dfrac{C_u}{FC}$ (or P/V ratio) | Degrees |
| 13 | Sales for Target Profit | $S_{target} = \dfrac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{P/V \text{ Ratio}}$ | Currency (₹) |
| 14 | Multi-Product BEP Weight | $BEP = \dfrac{FC}{\sum (W_i \times C_{u,i})}$ | Composite units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real engineering project offices, BEA is used for **make-or-buy decisions**, **pricing strategy formulation**, **plant shutdown analysis**, and **capacity planning**. It is a universal financial lingua franca across Civil, Mechanical, Electrical, and Industrial Engineering domains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Point Formula

We start with the fundamental **Profit Identity**:

$$\text{Profit } (P) = \text{Total Sales} - \text{Total Cost}$$

By the **cost classification principle**, the Total Cost equation is:

$$TC = FC + (v \times Q)$$

where `v` is the variable cost per unit and `Q` is the quantity sold. The Total Sales equation is:

$$S = p \times Q$$

where `p` is the selling price per unit. Substituting into the profit identity:

$$P = (p \times Q) - \big[FC + (v \times Q)\big]$$

Grouping the Q-terms together:

$$P = (p \times Q) - (v \times Q) - FC$$

Factoring out Q:

$$P = Q(p - v) - FC$$

Defining the **Contribution per unit** as $C_u = p - v$:

$$P = Q \cdot C_u - FC$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Insight:** The term $(Q \times C_u)$ represents the **Total Contribution**, which is the portion of sales revenue available to cover fixed costs and generate profit after variable costs are recovered.

---

### 3.1.1 Solving for Break-Even Quantity

At the Break-Even Point, by definition, **Profit = 0**. Substituting `P = 0`:

$$0 = Q_{BEP} \cdot C_u - FC$$

Rearranging:

$$Q_{BEP} \cdot C_u = FC$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{C_u} = \frac{FC}{p - v}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the break-even quantity in units.

---

### 3.1.2 Solving for Break-Even Sales (in Rupees)

To find the sales value at break-even, multiply $BEP_Q$ by the unit selling price `p`:

$$BEP_S = BEP_Q \times p$$

Substituting the derived expression:

$$BEP_S = \left(\frac{FC}{p - v}\right) \times p$$

Factoring:

$$BEP_S = FC \times \frac{p}{p - v}$$

Now, recall the **P/V Ratio** definition:

$$P/V = \frac{C}{S} = \frac{p - v}{p}$$

Taking the reciprocal:

$$\frac{p}{p - v} = \frac{1}{P/V}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{BEP_S = \frac{FC}{P/V \text{ Ratio}}}$$

This elegant result is the **second canonical KTU formula**, and it is especially useful when only sales values (not unit quantities) are given in a problem statement.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem (Model KTU Numerical — 14 marks weightage)**
>
> A small-scale engineering unit manufactures a specialized valve. The following data is provided:
>
> - **Selling Price (p)** = ₹200 per valve
> - **Variable Cost (v)** = ₹120 per valve
> - **Total Fixed Costs (FC)** = ₹4,00,000 per annum
> - **Actual Annual Sales** = 5,000 valves
>
> **Required:** Calculate (i) BEP in units, (ii) BEP in rupees, (iii) P/V Ratio, (iv) Margin of Safety in units and as a percentage, and (v) Actual Profit.

---

**Step 1 — Compute Contribution per unit:**

$$C_u = p - v = 200 - 120 = ₹80 \text{ per valve}$$

**Step 2 — Compute BEP in units:**

$$BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{C_u} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{80} = 5{,}000 \text{ valves}$$

**Step 3 — Compute BEP in Rupees:**

$$BEP_S = BEP_Q \times p = 5{,}000 \times 200 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute P/V Ratio:**

$$P/V = \frac{C_u}{p} \times 100 = \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute Margin of Safety in units:**

$$MoS_Q = \text{Actual Sales (units)} - BEP_Q = 5{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 0 \text{ valves}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Margin of Safety as a percentage:**

$$MoS\% = \frac{MoS_Q}{\text{Actual Sales (units)}} \times 100 = \frac{0}{5{,}000} \times 100 = 0\%$$

**Step 7 — Compute Actual Profit:**

$$P = (Q \times C_u) - FC = (5{,}000 \times 80) - 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The unit is operating at **exactly** the break-even point. Any sales below 5,000 valves will generate a **loss**, and the very first additional valve sold beyond 5,000 will yield a contribution of ₹80 (which is 40% pure profit since variable costs are already recovered). The MoS being zero indicates **zero cushion** and **maximum risk**.

---

### 3.3 Extended Numerical — Sales Required for a Target Profit

> [!NOTE]
> **Continued Problem:** Suppose the entrepreneur wants to earn a target profit of **₹1,60,000** per annum. How many valves must be sold?

**Step 1 — Apply the Target Profit formula:**

$$Q_{target} = \frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{C_u}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**
