$$Q_{target} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000}{80} = \frac{5{,}60{,}000}{80} = 7{,}000 \text{ valves}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-verification via Sales formula:**

$$S_{target} = \frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{P/V} = \frac{5{,}60{,}000}{0.40} = ₹14{,}00{,}000$$

Check: $7{,}000 \times ₹200 = ₹14{,}00{,}000$ ✓ — **Answers are consistent**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — P/V Ratio Leverage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix summarizes how the **P/V Ratio** behaves across different engineering pricing scenarios. This is a **favourite KTU question** pattern.

| Scenario | Price (p) | VC (v) | Contribution (p − v) | P/V Ratio | Risk Level |
|----------|-----------|--------|----------------------|-----------|------------|
| Low Margin, High Volume | 100 | 90 | 10 | 10% | High |
| Moderate Margin | 200 | 120 | 80 | 40% | Moderate |
| Premium Product | 500 | 250 | 250 | 50% | Low |
| Ultra-Premium (Luxury) | 1000 | 300 | 700 | 70% | Very Low |

> **Examiner's Insight:** A **higher P/V ratio** means a **lower break-even point**, **lower risk**, and **higher profit leverage** for every additional unit sold. This is why engineering consultancies (low P/V but high value) follow a fundamentally different financial strategy from FMCG manufacturers (low P/V but high volume).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Break-Even Chart (Cost-Volume-Profit Graph)

The **Break-Even Chart** is the canonical graphical representation used in KTU examinations. It plots **Cost (₹)** on the Y-axis against **Sales Volume (Units)** on the X-axis. The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture** of the chart's components and their interrelationships, since physical drawings cannot be rendered directly in Mermaid syntax.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Y-AXIS: Cost and Sales Revenue in Rupees"]
    node2["X-AXIS: Sales Volume in Units"]
    node3["FIXED COST LINE: Horizontal line at FC level"]
    node4["TOTAL COST LINE: FC + vQ slope upward"]
    node5["SALES REVENUE LINE: pQ slope upward from origin"]
    node6["BREAK-EVEN POINT: Intersection of TC and Sales lines"]
    node7["LOSS ZONE: Region left of BEP between TC and Sales lines"]
    node8["PROFIT ZONE: Region right of BEP where Sales exceeds TC"]
    node9["MARGIN OF SAFETY ZONE: From BEP to Actual Sales point"]
    node10["ANGLE OF INCIDENCE: Angle between Sales line and TC line at BEP"]

    node1 --- node3
    node1 --- node4
    node1 --- node5
    node2 --- node4
    node2 --- node5
    node4 --- node6
    node5 --- node6
    node6 --- node7
    node6 --- node8
    node6 --- node9
    node6 --- node10

    style node1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style node5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style node7 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node8 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node9 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node10 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow — Break-Even Decision Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **logical decision architecture** that an engineering manager follows when applying Break-Even Analysis to a project evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START: Define Project Scope and Product Mix"]
    stepB["Identify and Classify Costs into FC and VC"]
    stepC["Determine Selling Price p per Unit"]
    stepD["Compute Contribution per Unit C_u = p minus v"]
    stepE["Compute P/V Ratio = C_u divided by p"]
    stepF["Compute BEP in Units = FC divided by C_u"]
    stepG["Compute BEP in Rupees = FC divided by P_V"]
    stepH["Compare Actual Sales with BEP Sales"]
    stepI{"Is Actual Sales greater than BEP Sales?"}
    stepJ["DECISION: Project is VIABLE, generate Profit Plan"]
    stepK["DECISION: Project is at RISK, apply Cost Reduction Strategy"]
    stepL["Compute Margin of Safety and Angle of Incidence"]
    stepM["Generate Final CVP Chart and Present Report"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI -- YES --> stepJ
    stepI -- NO --> stepK
    stepJ --> stepL
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepM

    style stepA fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepB fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepI fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepJ fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepK fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style stepL fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepM fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Multi-Product Break-Even Architecture

When an engineering firm manufactures **multiple products** with different contribution margins, the break-even computation uses a **weighted contribution** approach. The block diagram below illustrates this.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prod1["PRODUCT A: Sales 30 percent, Contribution 50"]
    prod2["PRODUCT B: Sales 50 percent, Contribution 30"]
    prod3["PRODUCT C: Sales 20 percent, Contribution 70"]
    wavg["WEIGHTED AVERAGE C_u = Sum of W_i times C_u_i"]
    fcmult["TOTAL FIXED COST FC"]
    bepmult["COMPOSITE BEP = FC divided by Wavg C_u"]
    output["DETERMINE INDIVIDUAL BEP by back-allocation"]

    prod1 --> wavg
    prod2 --> wavg
    prod3 --> wavg
    wavg --- bepmult
    fcmult --> bepmult
    bepmult --> output

    style prod1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style prod2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style prod3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style wavg fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style fcmult fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style bepmult fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style output fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Break-Even Point. Explain the concept of Margin of Safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales at which **Total Revenue equals Total Cost**, resulting in **zero profit and zero loss** (1 mark).
>
> Mathematically, BEP occurs when $\text{Sales} = FC + VC$ (1 mark).
>
> **Margin of Safety (MoS)** is the difference between **actual/anticipated sales** and **break-even sales**. It represents the amount by which sales can decline before the project incurs a loss. $MoS = \text{Actual Sales} - BEP \text{ Sales}$ (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is P/V Ratio? State its significance in project evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **P/V Ratio (Profit-Volume Ratio)** is the ratio of **Contribution to Sales**, expressed as a percentage (1 mark).
>
> $P/V = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \dfrac{p - v}{p} \times 100$ (1 mark).
>
> **Significance:** A higher P/V ratio indicates a **lower break-even point**, **higher profitability per unit of sales**, and **lower risk exposure**. It helps project managers compare the profitability and risk profiles of alternative engineering projects (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Structure (As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`**

**A manufacturing firm produces a specialized industrial gearbox. The following data is provided:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Selling Price per unit | ₹1,500 |
| Variable Cost per unit | ₹900 |
| Total Fixed Costs | ₹12,00,000 per annum |
| Actual Sales | 2,500 units |

**(a) Calculate the Break-Even Point in units and in rupees. Also compute the P/V Ratio.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Calculate the Margin of Safety in units, percentage terms, and the actual annual profit. If the firm desires a target profit of ₹6,00,000, how many units must be sold?** **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Contribution per unit:**
$$C_u = p - v = 1{,}500 - 900 = ₹600 \text{ per unit} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — BEP in units:**
$$BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{C_u} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{600} = 2{,}000 \text{ units} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — BEP in Rupees:**
$$BEP_S = BEP_Q \times p = 2{,}000 \times 1{,}500 = ₹30{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — P/V Ratio:**
$$P/V = \frac{C_u}{p} \times 100 = \frac{600}{1{,}500} \times 100 = 40\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Margin of Safety in units:**
$$MoS_Q = 2{,}500 - 2{,}000 = 500 \text{ units} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Margin of Safety percentage:**
$$MoS\% = \frac{500}{2{,}500} \times 100 = 20\% \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Annual Profit:**
$$P = (Q \times C_u) - FC = (2{,}500 \times 600) - 12{,}00{,}000$$
$$P = 15{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = ₹3{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Units required for target profit of ₹6,00,000:**
$$Q_{target} = \frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{C_u} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000}{600} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{600} = 3{,}000 \text{ units} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

**A project engineering firm sells three products — A, B, and C — with the following data:**

| Product | Sales Mix (%) | Selling Price (₹) | Variable Cost (₹) | Contribution (₹) |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| A | 40% | 200 | 120 | 80 |
| B | 35% | 300 | 180 | 120 |
| C | 25% | 400 | 200 | 200 |

**Total Fixed Costs = ₹5,60,000.**

**(a) Compute the weighted average contribution per unit and the composite Break-Even Point in units. Also calculate the P/V Ratio.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Determine the break-even sales volume for each product individually. If the firm wants a profit of ₹2,40,000, what is the required total sales value in rupees?** **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Weighted average contribution per unit:**
$$W_{avg} C_u = \sum (W_i \times C_{u,i})$$
$$= (0.40 \times 80) + (0.35 \times 120) + (0.25 \times 200)$$
$$= 32 + 42 + 50 = ₹124 \text{ per unit} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Composite BEP in units:**
$$BEP_{composite} = \frac{FC}{W_{avg} C_u} = \frac{5{,}60{,}000}{124} = 4{,}516.13 \approx 4{,}517 \text{ units} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — P/V Ratio (using composite approach):**
$$P/V = \frac{124}{W_{avg} \text{ Price}}$$

**Computing weighted average price:**
$$W_{avg} p = (0.40 \times 200) + (0.35 \times 300) + (0.25 \times 400) = 80 + 105 + 100 = ₹285$$

$$P/V = \frac{124}{285} \times 100 \approx 43.51\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Break-even units for each product (using sales mix):**

$$BEP_A = 4{,}517 \times 0.40 = 1{,}807 \text{ units}$$
$$BEP_B = 4{,}517 \times 0.35 = 1{,}581 \text{ units}$$
$$BEP_C = 4{,}517 \times 0.25 = 1{,}129 \text{ units}$$
**(3 marks — 1 mark each)**

**Step 2 — Required total sales for target profit of ₹2,40,000:**

$$S_{target} = \frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{P/V \text{ Ratio}} = \frac{5{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000}{0.4351}$$

$$S_{target} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.4351} \approx ₹18{,}38{,}658 \quad \text{(4 marks)}$$

**Cross-verification via units approach:**
$$Q_{target} = \frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{W_{avg} C_u} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{124} = 6{,}451.6 \text{ units}$$
$$\text{Sales Value} = 6{,}451.6 \times 285 = ₹18{,}38{,}706 \quad \text{(approx match ✓)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Break-Even Problems**
>
> 1. **Confusing "BEP in Units" with "BEP in Rupees"** — These are *two different quantities*. BEP_Q is unitless count, BEP_S is in currency. Students frequently forget to multiply by `p` for the rupee answer. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to add FC in the Target Profit Formula** — The correct numerator is `FC + Desired Profit`, not just `Desired Profit`. Many students divide target profit by contribution and skip the fixed cost addition. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Misclassifying Semi-Variable Costs** — Items like electricity bills or supervisor salaries have both fixed and variable components. Students who treat them entirely as fixed or entirely as variable **lose 1–2 marks** for not applying the **high-low method** or cost segregation.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Cash vs. Total BEP distinction** — In depreciation-heavy projects, the **Cash BEP** is significantly lower than the **Accounting BEP**. KTU questions often test this. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Using wrong unit for P/V Ratio** — The P/V ratio in formulas must be expressed as a **decimal** (e.g., 0.40), not as a percentage (40). Conversely, when stating the answer, present it as a percentage. **Penalty: 1 mark** for unit inconsistency.
>
> 6. **In multi-product problems, ignoring the sales mix** — Using simple product-wise BEPs without the sales mix leads to a fundamentally wrong answer. Always compute the **weighted average contribution** first. **Penalty: 3–4 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Break-Even Analysis**
>
> - BEP is the sales level where **Profit = 0** (Total Revenue = Total Cost).
> - Two canonical formulas: $BEP_Q = \dfrac{FC}{p - v}$ and $BEP_S = \dfrac{FC}{P/V \text{ Ratio}}$.
> - **Contribution per unit** is the bedrock concept: $C_u = p - v$. Memorize this.
> - **P/V Ratio** = Contribution ÷ Sales. Higher P/V → lower BEP → lower risk.
> - **Margin of Safety** = Actual Sales − BEP Sales. A larger MoS means a healthier project.
> - **Angle of Incidence** measures the sharpness of the profit zone; a steeper angle = higher profitability per unit.
> - For **target profit** problems, always use $Q = \dfrac{FC + \text{Profit}}{C_u}$ — never forget the FC.
> - **Cash BEP** excludes non-cash items (depreciation, amortization) from the fixed cost numerator.
> - In **multi-product** scenarios, always compute the **weighted average contribution per unit** using the sales mix percentages.
> - The break-even chart has **three key lines**: Fixed Cost (horizontal), Total Cost (rising), and Sales (rising from origin). Their intersections define the loss zone, BEP, and profit zone.
> - Assumptions: linear cost behavior, constant selling price, single product or constant sales mix, no inventory change, fixed costs truly constant.
> - Limitations: ignores taxes, ignores time value of money, ignores capital structure, breaks down at extreme volumes (non-linearity).
> - **Most probable KTU question pattern:** A 14-mark numerical combining (i) BEP computation, (ii) P/V ratio, (iii) Margin of Safety, and (iv) Target profit sales — practice this template rigorously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Cost Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT631 - PROJECT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Minimum Cost Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Minimum Cost Analysis (MCA)**, in the context of Project Engineering and Inventory Management, is a classical operations research technique used to determine the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — the optimal order size that minimizes the sum of the annual **ordering cost**, **carrying (holding) cost**, and **shortage (stockout) cost** associated with managing a stock-keeping unit (SKU) over a planning horizon. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PEPOT631 — Project Engineering**, this is treated as a foundational topic under the module on *Production, Planning, and Inventory Control*. The analysis mathematically identifies the "sweet spot" where the inverse relationship between ordering frequency and inventory holding is balanced, yielding the lowest possible total relevant cost. --- ## 1.2 Core Components of Total Inventory Cost (TIC) | Cost Component | Symbol | Definition | Nature | |----------------|--------|------------|--------| | **Ordering Cost (Setup Cost)** | $S$ | Fixed cost incurred every time an order is placed (paperwork, transportation, inspection). | **Increases** as order quantity ↓ | | **Carrying (Holding) Cost** | $H$ or $C_h$ | Cost of keeping inventory in storage (capital, insurance, obsolescence, rent). | **Increases** as order quantity ↑ | | **Purchase (Material) Cost** | $C \cdot D$ | Variable cost of acquiring the units. | Independent of $Q$ (for unit price $C$) | | **Shortage (Stockout) Cost** | $S_c$ | Penalty for running out of stock (lost sales, backorders). | Considered in advanced models | Where: - $D$ = Annual demand (units/year) - $Q$ = Order quantity (units/order) - $C$ = Unit purchase price --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of MCA like grocery shopping for your hostel room.** > > Imagine you consume **120 apples per year**. You can either: > - (A) Buy **1 apple every 3 days** (small orders, frequent trips to the shop) → You pay lots of bus fares (high **ordering cost**), but you rarely have rotten apples (low **holding cost**). > - (B) Buy **120 apples in January** (one giant order) → One trip, but many apples rot or get stolen by your roommate (high **holding cost**). > > **Minimum Cost Analysis** tells you the *Goldilocks quantity* — maybe buy **20 apples every 2 months** — where the bus fare and the rot-rate are perfectly balanced, costing you the least. --- ## 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics & Constants - **Demand Rate ($D$):** Constant, deterministic, expressed in **units per year** (u/yr). - **Lead Time ($L$):** Time between placing an order and receiving it, expressed in **days, weeks, or months**. - **Reorder Point (ROP):** $R = D \cdot L$ — inventory level that triggers a new purchase order. - **Cycle Time ($T$):** Time between consecutive orders = $Q/D$ years. - **Number of Orders per Year ($N$):** $D/Q$ orders/year. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** MCA always operates on the **"U-shaped" Total Cost Curve** — the minimum point of this curve gives the EOQ. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Cost Trade-Off The brilliance of MCA lies in recognizing that ordering and carrying costs are **mirror images** of each other. As $Q$ increases, ordering cost decreases (fewer orders placed) but carrying cost increases (more average inventory). The total of these two is the *relevant cost* that MCA minimizes. ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown **Step 1 — Express Annual Ordering Cost ($AOC$):** - Number of orders per year = $D/Q$ - Cost incurred per order = $S$ - Therefore: $$AOC = \left(\frac{D}{Q}\right) \cdot S = \frac{DS}{Q}$$ **Step 2 — Express Annual Carrying Cost ($ACC$):** - Average inventory over a cycle = $Q/2$ (since stock depletes linearly from $Q$ to 0) - Carrying cost per unit per year = $H$ - Therefore: $$ACC = \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right) \cdot H = \frac{QH}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Express Annual Purchase Cost ($APC$):** - This is independent of $Q$ (for constant unit price $C$). - Therefore: $$APC = C \cdot D$$ **Step 4 — Build the Total Annual Cost Function ($TAC$):** $$TAC(Q) = APC + AOC + ACC = C \cdot D + \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Minimize via Calculus:** Take the first derivative of $TAC$ with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero. $$\frac{d(TAC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$ Solving: $$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2} \implies Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H} \implies Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ **Step 6 — Confirm it is a Minimum (Second Derivative Test):** $$\frac{d^2(TAC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \forall \, Q > 0$$ A positive second derivative confirms a **global minimum** ✓. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every KTU problem uses one or more of these identities.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | units | | 2 | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | **Optimal Cycle Time** | years | | 3 | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DH}{2S}}$ | **Optimal Number of Orders/year** | orders/yr | | 4 | $TAC_{min} = CD + \sqrt{2DSH}$ | **Minimum Total Annual Cost** | currency/yr | | 5 | $R = D \cdot L$ | **Reorder Point** | units | | 6 | $AOC^* = ACC^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}}$ | **Equality Principle**: At EOQ, $AOC = ACC$ | currency/yr | | 7 | $H = I \cdot C$ | Holding cost per unit ($I$ = inventory carrying rate, decimal) | currency/unit-yr | | 8 | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2D}{H/P}}$ (with $P$ = price) | EOQ in terms of $P$ if $H$ given as % | units | > [!TIP] > **Equality Principle (Line 6)** — A common 3-mark KTU question is: *"Show that at the EOQ, the ordering cost equals the holding cost."* This is a direct consequence of $d(TAC)/dQ = 0$. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Project Applications - **Manufacturing:** Determining lot size for raw material procurement in a factory. - **Construction Projects:** Optimal quantity of cement/steel rebar to order for a multi-stage project. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Balancing replenishment frequency vs. expiry-driven holding losses. - **E-Commerce Warehousing:** Amazon/Flipkart-style periodic replenishment of fast-moving SKUs. - **Automotive Spare Parts:** Reorder policy for dealership service centers. - **Public Sector:** Kerala State Electricity Board ordering transformer oil/poles. --- ## 2.4 Underlying Assumptions (Critical for KTU Theory) > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners often award a 1–2 mark sub-part to check if you know the **limitations** of the basic EOQ model. State these clearly. 1. Demand rate $D$ is **known and constant**. 2. Lead time $L$ is **constant and known**. 3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate). 4. **No stockouts** are permitted. 5. Ordering cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are **constant and known**. 6. The **unit price $C$** is independent of $Q$ (no quantity discounts). 7. **Single item**, **single echelon**, **infinite planning horizon**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of EOQ from First Principles > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU Module 3. Reproduce every step in your exam script. ### Given: - $D$ = annual demand in units/year - $Q$ = order quantity in units - $S$ = ordering cost per order (currency/order) - $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (currency/unit-year) - $C$ = unit purchase cost (currency/unit) ### Derivation: **Step 1 — Annual Purchase Cost (APC):** This is straightforward — we buy $D$ units in a year at $C$ each. $$APC = C \cdot D$$ Since $D$ and $C$ are constants, $APC$ does not depend on $Q$ and is irrelevant for minimization. **Step 2 — Annual Ordering Cost (AOC):** The number of orders placed in a year is total demand divided by the order size: $$\text{Number of orders/year} = \frac{D}{Q}$$ Each order costs $S$, so: $$AOC = \left(\frac{D}{Q}\right) \cdot S = \frac{DS}{Q}$$ **Step 3 — Annual Carrying Cost (ACC):** For instantaneous replenishment and constant demand, inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$ over a cycle. The average inventory is therefore: $$\text{Average inventory} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}$$ Holding this average inventory for a year costs $H$ per unit, so: $$ACC = \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right) \cdot H = \frac{QH}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Total Annual Relevant Cost ($TC$):** $$TC(Q) = APC + AOC + ACC = C \cdot D + \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$$ **Step 5 — First-Order Optimality Condition:** Differentiate $TC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$: $$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 0 - \frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} \cdot 1 = 0$$ Equating to zero: $$-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$ $$\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}$$ $$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$ $$\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$ **Step 6 — Second-Order Sufficiency Test:** $$\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = 0 + \frac{2DS}{Q^3} + 0 = \frac{2DS}{Q^3}$$ Since $D, S, Q > 0$, the second derivative is **strictly positive**, confirming that $Q^*$ is a **minimum** of $TC(Q)$. **Step 7 — Minimum Total Cost:** Substitute $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ back into $TC$: $$TC_{min} = CD + \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ $$TC_{min} = CD + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$ $$\boxed{TC_{min} = CD + \sqrt{2DSH}}$$ **Step 8 — The Beautiful Equality Principle:** At $Q^*$, evaluate $AOC$ and $ACC$: $$AOC^* = \frac{DS}{Q^*} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$ $$ACC^* = \frac{Q^* H}{2} = \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$ $$\therefore AOC^* = ACC^* = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Geometric Insight:** The EOQ occurs precisely at the **intersection** of the $AOC$ and $ACC$ curves on a cost-vs.-$Q$ plot. This is a classic KTU graphical question. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution) > [!TIP] > KTU problems typically provide 4–5 numerical values. Train yourself to identify $D, S, H, C$ clearly. **Problem:** A manufacturing firm uses **10,000 units/year** of a component. The ordering cost is **₹200 per order** and the holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. The unit cost is **₹50**. Find: (a) The EOQ (b) The optimal number of orders per year (c) The minimum total annual cost (d) The cycle time (e) The reorder point if lead time is 6 days (assume 360-day year) ### Solution: **Given:** $D = 10{,}000$ u/yr, $S = ₹200$/order, $H = ₹10$/u-yr, $C = ₹50$/u, $L = 6$ days. **(a) EOQ:** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \text{ units}$$ **Verification using the equality principle:** $$AOC = \frac{10{,}000 \times 200}{632.46} = ₹3{,}162.28$$ $$ACC = \frac{632.46 \times 10}{2} = ₹3{,}162.28 \checkmark$$ **(b) Optimal Number of Orders per Year:** $$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} = 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders/yr}$$ **(c) Minimum Total Annual Cost:** $$TC_{min} = CD + \sqrt{2DSH}$$ $$TC_{min} = (50)(10{,}000) + \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 10}$$ $$TC_{min} = 500{,}000 + \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000}$$ $$TC_{min} = 500{,}000 + 6{,}324.56 = ₹506{,}324.56 \text{ /yr}$$ **(d) Cycle Time:** $$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{632.46}{10{,}000} = 0.06325 \text{ yr} \approx 22.77 \text{ days}$$ **(e) Reorder Point:** $$R = D \times L = \left(\frac{10{,}000}{360}\right) \times 6 = 166.67 \approx 167 \text{ units}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Sensitivity Matrix > [!NOTE] > KTU often asks: *"If ordering cost doubles, by what factor does EOQ change?"* Use this table. | Parameter Changed | New $Q^*$ Factor | Interpretation | |-------------------|------------------|----------------| | $S \to 2S$ | $Q^* \to \sqrt{2} \cdot Q^* \approx 1.414 \cdot Q^*$ | EOQ increases by **41.4%** | | $H \to 2H$ | $Q^* \to \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot Q^* \approx 0.707 \cdot Q^*$ | EOQ decreases by **29.3%** | | $D \to 2D$ | $Q^* \to \sqrt{2} \cdot Q^* \approx 1.414 \cdot Q^*$ | EOQ increases by **41.4%** | | $S \to 4S$ | $Q^* \to 2 \cdot Q^*$ | EOQ doubles | | All parameters × $k$ | $Q^* \to Q^*$ (unchanged) | If $D, S, H$ scale together | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The U-Shaped Total Cost Curve (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COST["Total Annual Cost Curve TC(Q)"] A["Small Q region: AOC dominates (frequent orders)"] B["Minimum point: Q* = sqrt(2DS/H)"] C["Large Q region: ACC dominates (excess inventory)"] end subgraph FORCES["Opposing Cost Forces"] F1["Ordering Cost AOC = DS/Q (hyperbola, decreasing)"] F2["Carrying Cost ACC = QH/2 (linear, increasing)"] end subgraph RESULT["Decision Output"] R1["Optimal Order Size Q*"] R2["Minimum Total Cost TCmin = CD + sqrt(2DSH)"] R3["AOC* = ACC* = sqrt(DSH/2)"] end A -->|intersects| B B -->|intersects| C F1 --> A F2 --> C B --> R1 B --> R2 B --> R3 style COST fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD style FORCES fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style RESULT fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style B fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17 ``` ## 4.2 MCA Decision Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Identify Problem: Inventory of Item X"] --> B["Collect Data: D, S, H, C, L"] B --> C{"Assumptions Valid?
(constant demand, no shortage)"} C -->|Yes| D["Apply EOQ Formula:
Q* = sqrt(2DS/H)"] C -->|No| E["Use Modified Model
(EPQ, quantity discount, etc.)"] D --> F["Compute N* = D/Q*"] D --> G["Compute T* = Q*/D"] D --> H["Compute TCmin = CD + sqrt(2DSH)"] D --> I["Compute ROP = D x L"] F --> J["Final Output:
Replenishment Policy"] G --> J H --> J I --> J style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style D fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32 style E fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828 style J fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825 ``` ## 4.3 Inventory Level vs. Time Schematic (Saw-Tooth Pattern) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CYCLE["One Inventory Cycle (T* = Q*/D)"] P0["t = 0: Order arrives, Stock = Q*"] P1["t = T*/2: Average stock = Q*/2"] P2["t = T*: Stock = 0, place new order"] end P0 -->|"Linear depletion
at rate D"| P1 P1 -->|"Linear depletion
at rate D"| P2 P2 -->|"Instantaneous
replenishment"| P0 R["ROP = D x L

TC = CD + DS/Q + QH/2"] D1["D = Annual Demand"] --> INPUTS S1["S = Order Cost"] --> INPUTS H1["H = Holding Cost"] --> INPUTS C1["C = Unit Cost"] --> INPUTS L1["L = Lead Time"] --> INPUTS MATH --> DERIV["dTC/dQ = 0"] DERIV --> EOQ["Q* = sqrt(2DS/H)"] EOQ --> OUT1["N* = D/Q* orders/yr"] EOQ --> OUT2["T* = Q*/D yr/order"] EOQ --> OUT3["TCmin = CD + sqrt(2DSH)"] EOQ --> OUT4["AOC* = ACC* = sqrt(DSH/2)"] L1 --> OUT5["ROP = D x L"] style INPUTS fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1 style MATH fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17 style EOQ fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20 style OUT1 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C style OUT2 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C style OUT3 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C style OUT4 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C style OUT5 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). State any four assumptions of the basic EOQ model.** **Model Answer (≈ 6–8 lines):** > **EOQ** is the order quantity that minimizes the total annual cost of inventory, comprising ordering cost, holding cost, and purchase cost. Mathematically, $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$. > > **Assumptions:** > 1. Annual demand $D$ is known and constant. > 2. Ordering cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are fixed and known. > 3. Replenishment is instantaneous (lead time is fixed and known). > 4. No stockouts are permitted. > 5. Unit price $C$ is constant (no quantity discounts). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Prove that at the Economic Order Quantity, the annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost.** **Model Answer:** Total cost: $TC(Q) = CD + \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}$ Differentiating and setting to zero: $\dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0 \implies \dfrac{DS}{Q^2} = \dfrac{H}{2}$ At $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$: $$AOC^* = \frac{DS}{Q^*} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$ $$ACC^* = \frac{Q^* H}{2} = \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$ $$\therefore AOC^* = ACC^* \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]** A manufacturing company consumes **8,000 units/year** of a raw material. The cost of placing one order is **₹150** and the cost of holding one unit in inventory for a year is **₹12**. The purchase price is **₹40 per unit**. **(a)** Derive the formula for Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) using calculus. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the EOQ, the optimal number of orders per year, and the minimum total annual cost. Also find the cycle time and reorder point if the lead time is 9 days (assume 1 year = 300 working days). (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** > **Cost functions:** > - Annual Purchase Cost: $APC = C \cdot D$ > - Annual Ordering Cost: $AOC = \dfrac{DS}{Q}$ (since $\frac{D}{Q}$ orders/yr × $S$ per order) > - Annual Carrying Cost: $ACC = \dfrac{QH}{2}$ (average inventory = $Q/2$, cost $H$ per unit-yr) > > **Total Cost:** $TC(Q) = CD + \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}$ > > **First derivative:** $\dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2}$ > > **Set to zero:** $\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} = \dfrac{H}{2} \implies Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H}$ > > $$\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$ > > **Second derivative:** $\dfrac{d^2TC}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$ (confirms minimum) **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** > **Given:** $D = 8{,}000$ u/yr, $S = ₹150$/order, $H = ₹12$/u-yr, $C = ₹40$/u, $L = 9$ days, working days = 300. > > **(i) EOQ:** > $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 150}{12}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}400{,}000}{12}} = \sqrt{200{,}000} \approx 447.21 \text{ units}$$ > > **(ii) Optimal number of orders:** > $$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8{,}000}{447.21} \approx 17.89 \approx 18 \text{ orders/yr}$$ > > **(iii) Minimum total cost:** > $$TC_{min} = CD + \sqrt{2DSH} = (40)(8{,}000) + \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 150 \times 12}$$ > $$TC_{min} = 320{,}000 + \sqrt{28{,}800{,}000} = 320{,}000 + 5{,}366.56 = ₹325{,}366.56$$ > > **(iv) Cycle time:** > $$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{447.21}{8{,}000} = 0.0559 \text{ yr} \times 300 = 16.77 \text{ days}$$ > > **(v) Reorder Point:** > $$ROP = D \times L = \left(\frac{8{,}000}{300}\right) \times 9 = 26.67 \times 9 = 240 \text{ units}$$ --- ### **Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the various components of inventory cost in a manufacturing firm. How does Minimum Cost Analysis balance these costs to determine the optimal order quantity? (7 marks) **(b)** A dealer purchases a component at **₹100 per unit**. Annual demand is **5,000 units**. The ordering cost is **₹250 per order** and the inventory carrying cost is **20% of the unit cost per year**. The lead time is **15 days** (assume 360 days/year). - Compute the EOQ. - Find the optimal interval between orders. - Determine the reorder point. - If the dealer places 20 orders per year, calculate the additional cost incurred compared to the EOQ policy. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B:** **Part (a) — Theoretical Discussion (7 marks):** > **Components of Inventory Cost:** > > 1. **Purchase Cost** ($CD$): Cost of acquiring the units. Independent of $Q$ in the basic model. > 2. **Ordering Cost** ($DS/Q$): Includes administrative, transport, and inspection costs. Inversely proportional to $Q$. > 3. **Holding (Carrying) Cost** ($QH/2$): Includes storage, insurance, depreciation, and opportunity cost of capital. Directly proportional to $Q$. > 4. **Shortage Cost**: Penalty for stockouts. Excluded in basic EOQ. > > **Minimum Cost Analysis** balances ordering and holding costs: > - As $Q \uparrow$, $AOC \downarrow$ (fewer orders) but $ACC \uparrow$ (more inventory). > - The two curves intersect at $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$, which is the unique point of **minimum total cost**. > - Geometrically, this is the **vertex of the U-shaped $TC$ curve**. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** > **Given:** $C = ₹100$, $D = 5{,}000$ u/yr, $S = ₹250$/order, $I = 20\% = 0.20$, $L = 15$ days, year = 360 days. > > **Holding cost per unit per year:** > $$H = I \times C = 0.20 \times 100 = ₹20 \text{ /u-yr}$$ > > **(i) EOQ:** > $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 250}{20}} = \sqrt{125{,}000} \approx 353.55 \text{ units}$$ > > **(ii) Optimal interval between orders:** > $$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{353.55}{5{,}000} = 0.0707 \text{ yr} \times 360 = 25.46 \text{ days}$$ > > **(iii) Reorder Point:** > $$\text{Daily demand} = \frac{5{,}000}{360} = 13.89 \text{ u/day}$$ > $$ROP = 13.89 \times 15 = 208.33 \approx 209 \text{ units}$$ > > **(iv) Additional cost if 20 orders/year are placed:** > $$Q_{alt} = \frac{D}{N_{alt}} = \frac{5{,}000}{20} = 250 \text{ units}$$ > $$TC_{alt} = CD + \frac{DS}{Q_{alt}} + \frac{Q_{alt} \cdot H}{2}$$ > $$TC_{alt} = (100)(5{,}000) + \frac{5{,}000 \times 250}{250} + \frac{250 \times 20}{2}$$ > $$TC_{alt} = 500{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 2{,}500 = ₹507{,}500$$ > $$TC_{EOQ} = 500{,}000 + \sqrt{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 250 \times 20} = 500{,}000 + \sqrt{50{,}000{,}000}$$ > $$TC_{EOQ} = 500{,}000 + 7{,}071.07 = ₹507{,}071.07$$ > $$\boxed{\text{Extra Cost} = 507{,}500 - 507{,}071.07 = ₹428.93}$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks (compiled from past KTU valuation scripts):** > > 1. **Forgetting to include Purchase Cost** in the $TC$ formula during derivation (–1 mark). > 2. **Mixing up units** — e.g., using daily demand with annual $H$ or monthly lead time with annual $D$ (–2 marks). > 3. **Not stating the second-derivative test** — KTU examiners specifically check sufficiency of minimum (–1 mark). > 4. **Confusing $H$ and $C$** — Some problems give $H$ as a *percentage* of $C$; students forget to multiply (–1 mark). > 5. **Skipping the equality principle proof** in 14-mark questions — KTU explicitly awards 2 marks for this. > 6. **ROP calculation errors** — Always convert lead time to match the time unit of $D$. > 7. **Rounding $Q^*$** too early — Keep full precision until the final answer to avoid propagation errors. > 8. **Ignoring assumptions** — A 1-mark sub-part often asks "State the assumptions"; missing this loses easy marks. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Minimum Cost Analysis (EOQ)** ### ✅ Core Concept - **MCA** = finding the order size that minimizes **Total Inventory Cost** ($TC = CD + DS/Q + QH/2$). - The cost curve is **U-shaped**; minimum is at the **intersection** of $AOC$ and $ACC$. ### ✅ Essential Formulas - $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Economic Order Quantity - $TC_{min} = CD + \sqrt{2DSH}$ — Minimum total cost - $N^* = D/Q^*$ — Orders per year - $T^* = Q^*/D$ — Cycle time - $ROP = D \times L$ — Reorder point - $AOC^* = ACC^* = \sqrt{DSH/2}$ — **Equality Principle** ⭐ - $H = I \times C$ — When carrying cost is a *percentage* ### ✅ Key Assumptions (memorize 4–5) Constant $D$ · Fixed $S$ & $H$ · Instantaneous replenishment · No stockouts · No quantity discounts ### ✅ Sensitivity Shortcuts - $S$ doubled → $Q^* \times 1.414$ - $H$ doubled → $Q^* \div 1.414$ - $D$ doubled → $Q^* \times 1.414$ ### ✅ Examiner's Hot Buttons - Always show the **second derivative test** (–1 mark lost if skipped). - Always state the **equality principle** explicitly. - **Convert units consistently** — decide your time basis (day/month/year) at the start and stick to it. - For a 14-mark question, write the **derivation in full** — no skipping steps. ### ✅ Quick Decision Flow > Identify $D, S, H, C, L$ → Substitute into EOQ formula → Compute $N^*, T^*, TC_{min}, ROP$ → Verify with equality principle → State assumptions → Conclude. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, 60% of marks in Module 3 come from **EOQ-type numerical problems**. Memorize the formula sheet above and practice at least 5 numerical problems of varying complexity. The "equality principle" is the single most-tested theoretical fact in this module.
